messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the guide say about About applying ACLs to VLANs?

About applying ACLs to VLANs: ACLs can be applied to VLANs in the inbound (ingress) and outbound (egress) directions. Sequence numbering If no sequence number is specified, the software appends new ACEs to the end of the ACL with a sequence number equal to the highest ACE currently in the list plus 10. The sequence numbers may be resequenced using the access-list resequence command. Public ACL usage tips 13 ACL Logging On the 6200, 6300, and 6300L Switch Series: From 10.17 release onwards, ACL Logging will function properly on a VSF stack for packets received on a member that is not the conductor. The ACL name, sequence number, action, and other relevant details for the matching ACE will be logged. Deny ACLs If multiple ACLs of different types are applied in the same direction, a deny ACE, whether explicit or implicit, in one ACL overrides a permit ACL in another. A deny ACE is an ACE within an ACL that uses the deny action keyword.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the guide say about VLAN ACLs and interaction with VXLAN traffic?

VLAN ACLs and interaction with VXLAN traffic: NOTE This section applies to these AOS-CX platforms that support VXLAN and VXLAN ACLs: 5420, 6200, 6300, 6400, 8100, 8325, 8360, 9300/9300S, 10040, 10000. VLAN policies applied to an SVI will not match VXLAN traffic over a VNI. Public About address and port object groups 15 Interactions with VXLAN traffic on an L2 VNI About this task The way VLAN ACLs interact with VLAN traffic carried by VXLAN tunnels depends on the AOS-CX platform. For the purposes of this discussion, VLAN traffic is Ethernet traffic with an IP payload. VXLAN traffic includes a VXLAN header, encapsulating the Ethernet frame and IP header into the VXLAN UDP packet. VLAN traffic can be considered to be "normal" L2 traffic, and VXLAN traffic can be considered to be "tunneled" (encapsulated) L2 traffic.

topic: VLAN ACLs and interaction with VXLAN traffic

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the access-list copy command do?

The access-list copy command copies an IPv4, IPv6, or MAC ACL to a new destination ACL or overwrites an existing ACL. Specifies the type of ACL. Public access-list copy 24 Specifies the name of the ACL to be copied. <ACL-NAME> <DESTINATION-ACL> Specifies the name of the destination ACL. Syntax: access-list {ip|IPv6|mac} <ACL-NAME> copy <DESTINATION-ACL>.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the access-list log-timer command do?

The access-list log-timer command sets the log timer interval for all ACEs that have the log parameter configured. Resets the log timer to its default 300 seconds. Specifies the log timer interval in seconds. Range: 5 to 300. Syntax: access-list log-timer {default|<INTERVAL>}.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the access-list secure-update command do?

The access-list secure-update command this command determines if access lists are updated using the secure-update feature. Secure-update is enabled by default. When secure update is enabled and an ACL is updated or replaced, one or more override entries are installed in the TCAM table(s) containing the ACL that is being modified. As a result, all traffic of the same type as the currently configured ACL will be denied on the interfaces to which the ACL is applied. This ensures that traffic is not temporarily allowed while modifying an ACL. Upon completion of the update, the TCAM override entries are uninstalled and traffic resumes ACL matching. The no version of this command disables this feature. If secure-update is disabled, there will be no override entry installed. Syntax: access-list secure-update.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the apply access-list (to interface or LAG) command do?

The apply access-list (to interface or LAG) command applies an ACL to the interface (Individual front plane port) or Link Aggregation Group (LAG) identified by the current interface or LAG context. The no form of this command removes application of the ACL from the current interface or LAG identified by the current interface or LAG context. NOTE ACLs cannot be applied to an L3 port or subinterface on 6300 switch series: S3L75A, S3L76A, S3L77A. Specifies the ACL type: ip for IPv4, IPv6 for IPv6, or mac for MAC ACL. Specifies the ACL name. Public apply access-list (to interface or LAG) 79 in out. Syntax: no apply access-list {ip | IPv6 | mac} <ACL-NAME> {in | out}.

syntax: no apply access-list {ip | IPv6 | mac} <ACL-NAME> {in | out}

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the apply access-list (to interface VLAN) command do?

The apply access-list (to interface VLAN) command applies an ACL to the interface VLAN (or range of interface VLANs) identified by the current interface VLAN context. Using the apply access-list command on an interface VLAN interface with an already-applied ACL of the same direction and type will replace the currently-applied ACL. The no form of this command removes application of the ACL from the interface VLAN (or range of interface VLANs) identified by the current interface VLAN context. Specifies the ACL type: ip for IPv4, IPv6 for IPv6. Specifies the ACL name. Selects the routed inbound (routed ingress) traffic direction. Selects the routed outbound (routed egress) traffic direction. • Each ACL of a given type can be applied to the same interface VLAN once in each direction. Syntax: apply access-list {ip|IPv6} <ACL-NAME> {routed-in|routed-out}.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the apply access-list (to subinterface) command do?

The apply access-list (to subinterface) command applies an ACL to the current port or LAGsubinterface context or subinterface context range. Public apply access-list (to subinterface) 84 The no form of this command removes application of the ACL from the current port or LAGsubinterface context or subinterface context range. NOTE ACLs cannot be applied to an L3 port or subinterface on 6300 switch series: S3L75A, S3L76A, S3L77A. NOTE An ACL cannot be applied to the parent interface of one or more subinterfaces. This also means that a subinterface cannot be added to an interface if there is an ACL applied. NOTE ACE VLAN IDs cannot be added to ACLs applied to subinterfaces. This also means that an ACL with an ACE matching on a VLAN ID cannot be applied to a subinterface. Syntax: apply access-list {ip|IPv6|mac} <ACL-NAME> {in|out}.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the apply access-list (to L3 VNI) command do?

The apply access-list (to L3 VNI) command applies an ACL to the current L3 VNI. Only one direction (routed-in) and one type (IPv4/IPv6) of an ACL may be applied to an L3 VNI at a time, thus the apply command on an L3 VNI with an already applied ACL of the same direction and type replaces the currently-applied ACL. The no form of this command removes application of the ACL from the L3 VNI identified by the current L3 VNI context. NOTE ACLs cannot be applied to an L3 port or subinterface on 6300 switch series: S3L75A, S3L76A, S3L77A. Specifies the ACL type: ip for IPv4 or IPv6 for IPv6. Specifies the ACL name. Selects the routed-inbound (routed ingress) traffic direction. Syntax: apply access-list {ip|IPv6} <ACL-NAME> {routed-in}.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the object-group all reset command do?

The object-group all reset command resets the user configuration back to the active configuration for all object types (address and port). This command takes immediate effect, it is not saved in the user configuration. Use this command if misconfiguration of address object groups and port object groups has occurred. Individual address and port object groups can be reset respectively with the object-group address reset and object-group port reset commands. Syntax: object-group all reset.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the apply policy command do?

The apply policy command applies a policy to the global config context. Only one policy can be globally applied at a time. Applying a policy globally again, replaces the previous globally applied policy. The no form of this command removes application of the global policy. Specifies the policy to apply. in. messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the policy reset command do?

The policy reset command changes the user-specified policy configuration to match the active policy configuration. Use this command when a discrepancy exists between what the user configured and what is active and accepted by the system. Specifies the policy to be reset. <POLICY-NAME>. Syntax: policy <POLICY-NAME> reset.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the guide say about ACL and Policy hardware resource commands?

ACL and Policy hardware resource commands: Show Resources The show resources command allows users to see hardware resource consumption in the switch. These hardware resources include the following: • TCAM entries • L4 Port ranges • High-Capacity TCAM entries On the 6400 and 8400X switch series, these resources are only reported per line module. On the 4100i, 6000, 6100, 8320, 8325, and 8360 switch series, these resources are reported for the entire switch. On the 9300 switch series, resources are reported per pipe. The 9300 switch series has four pipes and the 9300s and 10040 switch series has two pipes. On the 6300, 6400, and 8360 switch series, when IPFIX is initially enabled, it always pre-reserves a large number of TCAM entries for learning new flows. On the 6200 and 6300 switch series, these resources are only reported per VSF member. The usage of high capacity TCAM can be viewed specifically using show system high-capacity-tcam. This command displays the feature that is currently configured, pending configuration, and the default feature present in high capacity TCAM.

topic: ACL and Policy hardware resource commands

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the show resources command do?

The show resources command on the HPE Aruba Networking 8100 and 8360 Switch Series, shows hardware resource consumption on the switch. Resource data is updated every 10 seconds. Hardware resource consumption information is shown for: • TCAM entries • Policers Public. Syntax: show resources [vsx-peer].

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip local-proxy-arp exclude {<ip-address>} command do?

The ip local-proxy-arp exclude {<ip-address>} command enables exemption of a specific IP address from local proxy ARP for the specified interface. This allows specified IP addresses to bypass local proxy ARP processing on the interface. The maximum IP addresses can be exempted from Local Proxy ARP per interface are 256. The maximum IP addresses can be exempted from Local proxy ARP across all interfaces are 256. The no version of this command disables exemption of a specific IP address from local proxy ARP for the specified interface, while local proxy ARP remains configured on the interface. This allows specified IP addresses to be processed by local proxy ARP on the interface. IP address to exclude from local proxy ARP. Syntax: no ip local-proxy-arp exclude {<ip-address>}.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip proxy-arp command do?

The ip proxy-arp command enables proxy ARP for the specified Layer 3 interface. Proxy ARP is supported on Layer 3 physical interfaces, LAG interfaces, and VLAN interfaces. It is disabled by default. To enable proxy ARP on an interface, routing must be enabled on that interface. The no form of this command disables proxy ARP for the specified interface. Public ip proxy-arp 289. Syntax: no ip proxy-arp.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the show arp inspection interface command do?

The show arp inspection interface command shows the current configuration of dynamic ARP inspection on an interface. Specifies the interface. Specifies the VLAN ID. Range: 1 to 4094. Shows the output from the VSX peer switch. If the switches do not have the VSX configuration or the ISL is down, the output f rom the VSX peer switch is not displayed. This parameter is avai lable on switches that support VSX. On the HPE Aruba Networking 6400 Switch Series, interface identification differs. Syntax: show arp inspection interface [<IFNAME>] [vlan <VLAN-ID>] [vsx-peer].

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the show arp inspection statistics command do?

The show arp inspection statistics command shows statistics about forwarded and dropped ARP packets. When <VLAN-ID> is not specified, information is shown for all configured VLANs. <VLAN-ID> vsx-peer Specifies the VLAN ID or range of IDs separated by a dash " - ". Range: 1 to 4094. Shows the output from the VSX peer switch. If the switches do not have the VSX configuration or the ISL is down, the output f rom the VSX peer switch is not displayed. This parameter is avai lable on switches that support VSX. Shows the current configuration of dynamic ARP inspection on a VLAN. When <VLAN-ID> is not specified, information is shown for all configured VLANs. Specifies the VLAN ID or range of IDs separated by a dash " - ". Range: 1 to 4094. Shows the output from the VSX peer switch. Syntax: show arp inspection statistics vlan [<VLAN-ID>] [vsx-peer].

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the bfd <IPv4-ADDR> command do?

The bfd <IPv4-ADDR> command enables BFD under VRRP for the specified IP address. BFD is asynchronous and echo mode is supported. The no form of this command disables BFD under VRRP for the specified IP address. Specifies the address on which to enable BFD in IPv4 format (x. x.x.x), where x is a decimal number from 0 to 255. <IPv4-ADDR>. Syntax: no bfd <IPv4-ADDR>.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the bfd all-interfaces command do?

The bfd all-interfaces command enables BFD on all OSPFv2 or OSPFv3 interfaces. The no form of this command disables BFD on all active OSPFv2/OSPFv3 or IPv4/IPv6 interfaces, excluding those on which BFD was enabled at the interface level with the commands ip ospf bfd and IPv6 o spfv3 bfd. Syntax:
- no bfd all-interfaces
- bfd all-interfaces (OSPF) 366.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the bfd echo disable command do?

The bfd echo disable command disables support for BFD echo packets. Echo packet support is enabled by default. Public bfd echo disable 372 The no form of this command enables support for BFD echo packets. NOTE BFD IPv6 Echo is not supported. Syntax: no bfd echo disable.

source_file: 6300_6400/10_17_1000/Command-line interface Reference Guide For 6300 and 6400 Switch Series.md

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the bfd echo-src-ip-address <IPv4-ADDR> command do?

The bfd echo-src-ip-address <IPv4-ADDR> command sets the source IPv4 address for BFD echo packets. This address is used in all echo sessions. NOTE The source IP address must not be on the same network segment as any switch interface, otherwise a large number of ICMP redirect packets may be sent by the remote device, causing network congestion. The no form of this command removes the source IPv4 address for BFD echo packets, which causes the switch to stop sending echo packets. When a valid value is set, all sessions with a peer that is capable of receiving echo packets, will start transmitting echo packets. BFD control sessions continue to run concurrently with echo packets. Specifies an IP address in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), where x is a de cimal number from 0 to 255. <IPv4-ADDR> Public bfd echo-src-ip-address 374. Syntax: no bfd echo-src-ip-address <IPv4-ADDR>.

source_file: 6300_6400/10_17_1000/Command-line interface Reference Guide For 6300 and 6400 Switch Series.md

The syntax of the bfd echo-src-ip-address <IPv4-ADDR> command is: no bfd echo-src-ip-address <IPv4-ADDR>

document_title: Command Line Interface Reference Guide For 6300 And 6400 Switch Series
source_file: 6300_6400/10_17_1000/Command-line interface Reference Guide For 6300 and 6400 Switch Series.md

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the bfd min-echo-receive-interval <INTERVAL> command do?

The bfd min-echo-receive-interval <INTERVAL> command sets the minimum time interval between received BFD echo packets. Public bfd min-echo-receive-interval 375 The no form of this command removes the configured BFD echo packets interval. If the interval is not set, the default interval is used. NOTE BFD IPv6 Echo is not supported. Specifies the minimum reception interval in milliseconds. A valu e of 0 means that the switch does not support reception of BFD echo packets. Range: 0, 50 to 1000. Default: 500. <INTERVAL>. Syntax: no bfd min-echo-receive-interval <INTERVAL>.

source_file: 6300_6400/10_17_1000/Command-line interface Reference Guide For 6300 and 6400 Switch Series.md

The syntax of the bfd min-echo-receive-interval <INTERVAL> command is: no bfd min-echo-receive-interval <INTERVAL>

source_file: 6300_6400/10_17_1000/Command-line interface Reference Guide For 6300 and 6400 Switch Series.md

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the bfd min-receive-interval <INTERVAL> command do?

The bfd min-receive-interval <INTERVAL> command sets the minimum time interval between received BFD control packets on an interface. The no form of this command removes the configured BFD minimum interval on an interface. If the interval is not set, the default interval is used. Specifies the minimum receive interval in milliseconds. A value of 0 means that the switch does not support reception of BFD c ontrol packets. Range: 500 to 20000. Default: 3000. <INTERVAL>. Syntax: no bfd min-receive-interval <INTERVAL>.

source_file: 6300_6400/10_17_1000/Command-line interface Reference Guide For 6300 and 6400 Switch Series.md

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 ospfv3 bfd disable command do?

The IPv6 ospfv3 bfd disable command disables BFD on the current OSPFv3 interface. This overrides the global settings defined with the command bfd all-interfaces. Enables BGP to register with BFD to receive fast peering session deactivation messages from BFD. The no form of this command disables BGP for BFD. NOTE BFD is supported with IPv6 neighbors on the 6300, 6400, 8100, 8320, 8325, 8360, 8400, 9xxx and 100xx switch series. Specifies an IP address in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), where x is a de cimal number from 0 to 255. <IP-ADDR> <PEER-GROUP-NAME> Enables BFD for OSPFv3 AF on the current interface. The interface must have OSPFv3 AF enabled on it. The no form of this command disables BFD on the current interface. NOTE OSPFv3 AF IPv4 sessions are established through the IPv6 link-local address. Syntax: IPv6 ospfv3 bfd disable.

The syntax of the IPv6 ospfv3 bfd disable command is: IPv6 ospfv3 bfd disable.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the vrf <VRF-NAME> command do?

The vrf <VRF-NAME> command creates a VRF instance named <VRF-NAME> and then enters its context. Use default for <VRF-NAME> to enter the default VRF configure context. Public vrf 582 Except for the default VRF, the no form of the command deletes the named VRF instance and any IP configuration for interfaces or SVI linked to default VRF. The default VRF cannot be deleted and a warning is given if attempted. To erase the Route-Distinguisher and Route-Targets, enter the default VRF context and delete them manually one by one. NOTE Only default and mangement VRFs are supported on the following 6300 switches: S3L75A, S3L76A, S3L77A. Specifies the VRF name. Range: Up to 32 alphanumeric chara cters. The mgmt VRF cannot be used. <VRF-NAME>. Syntax: no vrf <VRF-NAME>.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip pim-bidir command do?

The ip pim-bidir command enables or disables PIM-Bidir in the designated interface. This command works in the interface context, including loopback. The IP address must be configured on the interface to enable BIDIR-PIM. Enables PIM Bidirectional on the interface. Public BIDIR-PIM commands 584 Disables PIM Bidirectional on the interface. Syntax: ip pim-bidir [enable | disable].

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip pim-bidir hello-delay <DELAY-VALUE> command do?

The ip pim-bidir hello-delay <DELAY-VALUE> command changes the maximum time, in seconds, before the router transmits the initial PIM hello message on the current interface. The no form of this command removes the currently configured value and sets it to default. The default value is 5. Configures the given value as the hello interval. Range: <0-5>. <DELAY-VALUE>. Syntax: no ip pim-bidir hello-delay <DELAY-VALUE>.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip pim-bidir hello-interval <interval-value> command do?

The ip pim-bidir hello-interval <interval-value> command changes the frequency at which the router transmits PIM hello messages on the current subnet. The router uses hello packets to inform neighbor routers of its presence and to comput the hello hold time. Hello hold time informs neighbor routers estimated wait time for the next hello packet from the router. Shortening the hello interval reduces the hello hold time. Default is set to 30 seconds. The no form of this command resets the frequency of PIM hello messages back to the default value on the current subnet. Public ip pim-bidir hello-interval 587 Configures the given value as hello interval. Range: 1-300 seco nds. • The default value (30 seconds) covers most deployment needs; change only for explicit convergence or timing requirements. Syntax: no ip pim-bidir hello-interval <interval-value>.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip pim-bidir hello-holdtime <holdtime-value> command do?

The ip pim-bidir hello-holdtime <holdtime-value> command configures the Hello Hold time (in seconds) advertised in PIM Hello messages on this interface; this overrides the default value derived from the current hello-interval. By default, the hold time that a neighbor uses before declaring this router down is automatically calculated (typically a multiple of the configured hello-interval). Manually configuring the hello-holdtime allows finer control of neighbor timeout detection. A smaller value can speed convergence (neighbors age out faster if hellos stop) but increases sensitivity to transient loss; a larger value provides stability at the cost of slower failure detection. Syntax: no ip pim-bidir hello-holdtime <holdtime-value>.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip pim-bidir override-interval <INTERVAL-VALUE> command do?

The ip pim-bidir override-interval <INTERVAL-VALUE> command configures the override interval that gets inserted into the Override Interval field of a LAN prune delay option. The no form of this command removes the currently configured value and sets it to the default. The default value is 2500 milliseconds. <INTERVAL-VALUE> Specifies override interval value. Range: 500-6000. Default: 2500. Syntax:
- no ip pim-bidir override-interval <INTERVAL-VALUE>
- ip pim-bidir override-interval 592.

- ip pim-bidir override-interval 592

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 pim6-bidir lan-prune-delay command do?

The IPv6 pim6-bidir lan-prune-delay command enables the LAN prune delay option on the current interface. With lan-prune-delay enabled, the router informs downstream neighbors on the wait time before pruning a flow after receiving a prune request. The no form of this command disables the LAN prune delay option on the current interface. Syntax: no IPv6 pim6-bidir lan-prune-delay.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the diag cable-diagnostic command do?

The diag cable-diagnostic command provides information about the cable health after running a diagnostic test on an interface. If you run a new cable diagnostic command when a cable diagnostic is in progress for the interface, the new cable diagnostic command fails to execute. In such a scenario, an error message is displayed. Public Cable diagnostic commands 629 On executing a cable diagnostic test command, it automatically clears the old test results before the new test starts. Specifies the name of the interface. <IF-NAME> test <IF-NAME> Runs a cable diagnostic test on an interface. show <IF-NAME> clear <IF-NAME>. Syntax: diag cable-diagnostic.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the copy checkpoint <CHECKPOINT-NAME> command do?

The copy checkpoint <CHECKPOINT-NAME> command copies an existing checkpoint configuration to a USB drive. The file format is defined when the checkpoint was created. <CHECKPOINT-NAME> Specifies the name of the checkpoint to copy. The checkpoint n ame can be alphanumeric. It can also contain underscores (_) an d dashes (-). <STORAGE-URL>> The USB drive must be formatted with the FAT file system. Specifies the name of the target file on the USB drive using the following syntax: usb: /<FILE> Copies a remote configuration file to a checkpoint. The remote configuration file must be in checkpoint format. Syntax: copy <REMOTE-URL> checkpoint <CHECKPOINT-NAME> [vrf <VRF-NAME>].

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the apply policy <POLICY-NAME> routed-in command do?

The apply policy <POLICY-NAME> routed-in command applies a policy to the current physical interface port or LAG or VLAN context. Subinterfaces are supported on interfaces and LAGs. Public apply policy (config-if, config-lag-if, config-if-... 698 a p p l y p o l i c y (c o n fi g - i f, c o n fi g - l a g - i f, c o n fi g - i f -... Only one direction of a policy can be applied to an interface or VLAN at a time, thus using the apply command on an interface or VLAN with an already-applied policy of the same direction will replace the currently applied policy. NOTE The VLAN context supports the in and out directions, which apply to both bridged and routed traffic. The Interface VLAN context only supports the routed-in direction which applies only to routed traffic. Syntax: no apply policy <POLICY-NAME> routed-in.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the class mac command do?

The class mac command creates or modifies a MAC traffic class to match specified packets. Class is composed of one or more class entries ordered and prioritized by sequence numbers. With this command, each class can classify traffic based on MAC header information. The no form of the command can be used to delete either a MAC traffic class (use no with the class command) or an individual MAC traffic class entry (use no with the sequence number). Specifies create or modify a MAC class. Specifies the name of this class. Specifies a sequence number for the class entry. Optional. Rang e: 1-4294967295. {match|ignore} Creates a rule to match or ignore specified packets. comment Stores the remaining entered text as a class comment. Syntax: class mac <CLASS-NAME>.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the aaa authentication port-access allow-cdp-auth command do?

The aaa authentication port-access allow-cdp-auth command use this command to allow or block authentication on the CDP (Cisco Discovery Protocol) BPDU (Bridge Protocol Data Unit). This is allowed by default. The no form of this command prevents authentication on CDP packets received on the port. This command can be issued from the interface (config-if) or Link Aggregation Group (config-lag-if) contexts. Syntax: no aaa authentication port-access allow-cdp-auth.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the aaa authentication port-access allow-cdp-bpdu command do?

The aaa authentication port-access allow-cdp-bpdu command allows all packets related to the CDP (Cisco Discovery Protocol) BPDU (Bridge Protocol Data Unit) on a secure port or LAG. This command can be issued from the interface (config-if) or Link Aggregation Group (config-lag-if) contexts. The no form of this command blocks the CDP BPDU on a secure port. On a nonsecure port, the command has no effect. Syntax: no aaa authentication port-access allow-cdp-bpdu.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the aaa authentication port-access allow-cdp-proxy-logoff command do?

The aaa authentication port-access allow-cdp-proxy-logoff command allows a client to be logged off from the system via a special TLV in the CDP packet. By default, proxy logoff via CDP packet support is disabled. When allow-cdp-proxy-logoff is enabled, TLV received from CDP packets corresponding to logoff processing will be read and logoff is issued to the clients. This only works on client authentication enabled ports and aaa authentication port-access allow-cdp-bpdu must be enabled to process. This command can be issued from the interface (config-if) or Link Aggregation Group (config-lag-if) contexts. Syntax: no aaa authentication port-access allow-cdp-proxy-logoff.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip bootp-gateway <IPv4-ADDR> command do?

The ip bootp-gateway <IPv4-ADDR> command configures a gateway address for the DHCP relay agent to use for DHCP requests. By default DHCP relay agent picks the lowest-numbered IP address on the interface. The no form of this command removes the gateway address. Specifies the IP address of the gateway in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), where x is a is a decimal number from 0 to 255. <IPv4-ADDR>. Syntax: no ip bootp-gateway <IPv4-ADDR>.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip dns domain-name <DOMAIN-NAME> [ vrf <VRF-NAME> ] command do?

The ip dns domain-name <DOMAIN-NAME> [ vrf <VRF-NAME> ] command configures a domain name that is appended to the DNS request. The domain can be either IPv4 or IPv6. By default, requests are forwarded on the default VRF. If a domain list is defined with the command ip dns domain-list, the domain name defined with this command is ignored. The no form of this command removes the domain name. Specifies the domain name to append to DNS requests. Length: 1 to 256 characters. <DOMAIN-NAME> vrf <VRF-NAME> Specifies a VRF name. Default: default. Syntax: no ip dns domain-name <DOMAIN-NAME> [ vrf <VRF-NAME> ].

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip dns host <HOST-NAME> command do?

The ip dns host <HOST-NAME> command associates a static IP address with a hostname. The DNS client returns this IP address instead of querying a DNS server for an IP address for the hostname. Up to six hosts can be defined. If no VRF is defined, the default VRF is used. The no form of this command removes a static IP address associated with a hostname. host <HOST-NAME> Specifies the name of a host. Length: 1 to 256 characters. <IP-ADDR> Specifies an IP address in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), where x is a de cimal number from 0 to 255, or IPv6 format (xxxx: xxxx: x xxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx), where x is a hexa decimal number from 0 to F. vrf <VRF-NAME> Specifies a VRF name. Default: default. Public ip dns host 1137. Syntax: no ip dns host <HOST-NAME>.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the erps ring <RINGID> command do?

The erps ring <RINGID> command this command creates an ERPS ring with a given ID. The no form of this command removes all the configurations of the ring, including instances. Required, specifies the ID of the ring. Range: 1-239 <RINGID> This command configures the ERPS ring member port. An L2 interface in the switch is associated to one of the two member ports of an ERPS ring. In case of an interconnection node, only port0 is applicable for the sub-ring. The no form of this command removes the association of the ring port to the L2 interface on the switch. Required, specifies the ID of the ring. Range: 1-239 <RINGID> <PORT0> <PORT1> <ifname> This command adds descriptive information to help administrators and operators understand the purpose of a ring. 1-64 printable ASCII characters are allowed. Syntax: erps ring <RINGID>.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the erps ring <RINGID> instance <ID> description command do?

The erps ring <RINGID> instance <ID> description command this command adds descriptive information to help administrators and operators understand the purpose of a ring instance. 1-64 printable ASCII characters are allowed. The no form of this command removes the ring instance description. Required, specifies the ID of the ring. Range: 1-239 <RINGID> <ID> <LINE> This configuration enables protection switching on the given instance of the ring. It is disabled by default. The no form of this command disables protection switching on the given instance of the ring. Required, specifies the ID of the ring. Range: 1-239 <RINGID> <ID> Required, specifies the ERPS ring instance identifier. Range: 1 -2. Public erps ring <RINGID> instance <ID> enable 1173. Syntax: erps ring <RINGID>.

source_file: 6300_6400/10_17_1000/Command-line interface Reference Guide For 6300 and 6400 Switch Series.md

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the erps ring <RINGID> instance <ID> revertive command do?

The erps ring <RINGID> instance <ID> revertive command configures the default revertive mode of operation for an ERPS ring. In revertive operation, after the conditions causing protection switching are cleared, traffic channels are restored to the recovered link blocking the RPL. This configuration is meaningful only on the RPL node. The no form of this command configures non-revertive mode of operation for an ERPS ring. In non-revertive operation, the traffic channels continue to use the RPL, if it has not failed, after conditions causing protection switching are cleared. This configuration is meaningful only on the RPL node. Required, specifies the ID of the ring. Range: 1-239 <RINGID> <ID> In ERPS, there is a central node called RPL Owner Node which blocks one of the ports to ensure that there is no loop formed for the Ethernet traffic. Syntax: erps ring <RINGID>.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the erps ring <RINGID> meg-level command do?

The erps ring <RINGID> meg-level command the R-APS messages transmitted by ERPS take the form of OAM PDUs as defined in G.8013. Each OAM PDU is transmitted at a specified level known as the Maintenance Entity Group (MEG) level. This command configures the level with which the ERPS packets must be transmitted. The no form of this command removes the configured MEG level and sets it to the default value of 7. Required, specifies the ID of the ring. Range: 1-239 Required, specifies the meg-level. Range: 0-7. Default: 7. <RINGID> <0-7> This command associates a sub-ring to a parent-ring and is required for the sub-ring to notify the parent- ring on change in topology. The no form of this command removes the parent ring identifier. Required, specifies the ID of the ring. Syntax: erps ring <RINGID>.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the show evpn mac-ip evi command do?

The show evpn mac-ip evi command show the information about the EVPN MAC-IP for the particular EVPN instance. Specifies the EVPN instance ID. <EVI-ID> Shows the information about the MLD group memberships learned through EVPN. A default SMET route is identified by the Source and Group addresses set to 0: : 0 and is applicable for both IP versions. Specifies the EVPN instance ID. Range: 1-16777214. <EVI-ID> Shows the remote VTEPs MAC-IP binding. The state of the peer VTEP denotes whether VXLAN tunnel to the VTEP is Up or Down. Display information for all VRFs. Specify a VRF by VRF name (if no <VRF-NAME> is specifie d, the default VRF is implied. Shows all EVPN configurations. Configures the virtual MAC address for EVPN. The no form of this command removes the virtual MAC address configuration. Syntax: show evpn mac-ip evi <EVI-ID>.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the vlan <ID> command do?

The vlan <ID> command specifies the VLAN ID and enters the VLAN context under EVPN. The no form of this command removes this configuration. Specifies the VLAN ID. Range: 2 - 4040. <ID>. Syntax: no vlan <ID>.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the vlan-ethernet-tag <VLAN-ID> {ethernet tag} command do?

The vlan-ethernet-tag <VLAN-ID> {ethernet tag} command associates the Ethernet Tag ID value to VLANs of the VLAN aware bundles. The no form of this command removes this configuration. Specifies the VLAN to which the ethernet tag is configuring. Ra nge: 1-4094. Public vlan-ethernet-tag (evpn vlan-aware-bundles) 1259 Specifies the non-default ethernet tag for the VLAN. Range: 1 -16777215. The Ethernet Tag ID value should be unique and associated to only one VLAN of the VLAN aware bundle. The configuration of this command is not mandatory, but may be required for interoperability with some third-party vendors. Syntax: no vlan-ethernet-tag <VLAN-ID> {ethernet tag}.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the redundancy switchover command do?

The redundancy switchover command causes the switch to immediately switch over to the Standby Management Module. This command must be executed from the Active Management Module and will fail if the Standby Management Module is in a failed state or not present. Syntax: redundancy switchover.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip icmp unreachable command do?

The ip icmp unreachable command enables the sending of ICMPv4 and ICMPv6 destination unreachable messages on the switch to a source host when a specific host is unreachable. The unreachable host address originates from the failed packed. Default setting. The no form of this command disables the sending of ICMPv4 and ICMPv6 destination unreachable messages from the switch to a source host when a specific host is unreachable. This command does not prevent other hosts from sending an ICMP unreachable message. Syntax: no ip icmp unreachable.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip igmp apply access-list <ACL-NAME> command do?

The ip igmp apply access-list <ACL-NAME> command configures the ACL on a particular interface to filter the IGMP join or leave packets based on rules set in the particular ACL name. The no form of this command unconfigures the rules set for the ACL. NOTE This configuration will override the ACL associated with IGMP snooping on the corresponding L2 VLAN. Associates an ACL with the IGMP. Specifies the name of the ACL. Syntax: no ip igmp apply access-list <ACL-NAME>.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip igmp last-member-query-interval <INTERVAL-VALUE> command do?

The ip igmp last-member-query-interval <INTERVAL-VALUE> command configures an IGMP last member query interval value in seconds on an interface, depending on the command context you are in. The no form of this command sets the value to a default of 1 second on an interface. Specifies an IGMP last-member-query-interval on the inter face. Default: 1 second. Range: 1-2 seconds. <INTERVAL-VALUE>. Syntax: no ip igmp last-member-query-interval <INTERVAL-VALUE>.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip igmp querier-wait-time <QUERIER-WAIT-TIME> command do?

The ip igmp querier-wait-time <QUERIER-WAIT-TIME> command configures initial IGMP querier-wait-time value in seconds. The no form of this command sets the IGMP querier-wait-time to the default value of 260 seconds. Note that the wait timer can be configured to any numbers within the 1-300 second range. Public ip igmp querier-wait-time 1468 Configures IGMP querier-wait-time to desired value. <QUERIER-WAIT-TIME>. Syntax: no ip igmp querier-wait-time <QUERIER-WAIT-TIME>.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip igmp static-group <MULTICAST-GROUP-IP> command do?

The ip igmp static-group <MULTICAST-GROUP-IP> command configures an IGMP static multicast group on an interface, depending on the command context you are in. You can configure a maximum of 32 IGMP static groups. The no form of the command unconfigures IGMP static multicast group on an interface. Specifies an IGMP static multicast group IP address on the inter face. Format: A. B. C. D <MULTICAST-GROUP-IP>. Syntax: no ip igmp static-group <MULTICAST-GROUP-IP>.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip igmp version <VERSION> command do?

The ip igmp version <VERSION> command configures the IGMP version on an interface, depending on the command context you are in. The no form of the command configures the default IGMP version, 3, on the interface. Specifies the IGMP version on the interface. Select 2 for IGMPv 2 (RFC2236). Select 3 for IGMPv3 (RFC3376). Values: 2 or 3. <VERSION>. Syntax: no ip igmp version <VERSION>.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip igmp version <VERSION> strict command do?

The ip igmp version <VERSION> strict command configures an IGMP strict version on an interface, depending on the command context you are in. Drops packets that do not match the configured version. The no form of the command removes the strict version configuration from the interface. Specifies the IGMP version on the interface. Select 2 for IGMPv 2 (RFC2236). Select 3 for IGMPv3 (RFC3376). Values: 2 or 3. Public ip igmp version strict 1476. Syntax: no ip igmp version <VERSION> strict.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip igmp snooping all-vlans command do?

The ip igmp snooping all-vlans command enable IGMP snooping on all VLANs. This configuration setting enables IGMP snooping on all existing VLANs, as well as on any VLANs that are created after the ip igmp snooping all-vlans command is issued. Public ip igmp snooping all-vlans 1514 When IGMP snooping is enabled globally, IGMP snooping can not be configured on individual VLANs within the VLAN context of the command-line interface. The no form of this command disables global IGMP snooping, allows IGMP snooping to remain enabled at the VLAN level, and allows the configuration of VLAN-level IGMP snooping settings. Syntax: no ip igmp snooping all-vlans.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the interface loopback <ID> command do?

The interface loopback <ID> command creates a loopback interface and changes to the config-loopback-if context. Loopback interfaces are layer 3. The no form of this command deletes a loopback interface. Specifies the loopback interface ID. Range: 1 to 256 <INSTANCE>. Syntax: no interface loopback <ID>.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip tcp mss <VALUE> command do?

The ip tcp mss <VALUE> command sets the TCP (Transmission Control Protocol) MSS (Maximum Segment Size) for an interface. When configured, the MSS option in the TCP header is set in the TCP handshake for negotiation during connection establishment. Once the TCP session is established, changing the TCP MSS value on the interface will not affect the existing session. The no form of this command removes the TCP MSS configuration from the interface. NOTE TCP MSS is supported on ROP, SVI, subinterface, and L3 GRE tunnel interface. Specifies the IPv4 TCP MSS in bytes. Range: 68 to 65495. Public ip tcp mss 1551. Syntax: no ip tcp mss <VALUE>.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 prefix-priority command do?

The IPv6 prefix-priority command configures custom IPv6 route prefix lengths for the exact prefix match tables of the switch. The switch must be rebooted to apply the change. NOTE Following the reboot, IPv6 prefix priorities will remain in a pending state until at least one route is learned. A connected route counts so this can be as simple as having an L3 interface with an IP address in the up state. The no form of this command resets the prefix lengths to their default <PREFIX-LENGTHS>> Specifies a space-separated list of exactly five or six prefix le ngths, in descending order. Range: 8 to 64. On the 6300 Switc h Series, six prefix lengths are used. On the 6400 Switch Series, six prefix lengths are used for profiles default and V2-defau lt, and five prefix lengths are used for profile v2-Core-High -Bandwidth. Syntax: IPv6 prefix-priority <PREFIX-LENGTHS>.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip-sla <IP-SLA-NAME> command do?

The ip-sla <IP-SLA-NAME> command creates an IP Service Level Agreement (SLA) profile and switches to the config-ip-sla context. The no form of this command deletes an IP-SLA profile. By default, all profile use the default VRF (default). Specifies an IP-SLA profile name. Length: 1 to 64 characters. <IP-SLA-NAME> Selects the IP-SLA responder. The responder can be configured for udp-echo, tcp-connect, udp-jitter-voip type. It requires the SLA name, SLA type, and port number as arguments. The no form of this command removes the IP-SLA responder. Specifies the IP-SLA responder name. Length: 1 to 64 chara cters. <SLA-NAME> udp-echo tcp-connect Enables responder for udp-echo probes. Selects TCP connect as the IP-SLA test mechanism. udp-jitter-voip Selects VOIP jitter as the IP-SLA test mechanism. Syntax: ip-sla responder <SLA-NAME> (udp-echo | tcp-connect | udp-jitter-voip).

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip address <IPv4-ADDR>/<MASK> command do?

The ip address <IPv4-ADDR>/<MASK> command sets the local IP address of a GRE tunnel. This address identifies the tunnel interface for routing. It must be on the same subnet as the tunnel address assigned on the remote device. The no form of this command deletes the local IP address assigned to a GRE tunnel. Specifies the tunnel IP address in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), where x is a decimal number from 0 to 255. You can remove leading ze ros. For example, the address 192.169.005.100 becomes 192. 168.5.100. Specifies the number of bits in the address mask in CIDR format (x), where x is a decimal number from 0 to 32. <IPv4-ADDR> <MASK>. Syntax: no ip address <IPv4-ADDR>/<MASK>.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the vrf attach <VRF-NAME> command do?

The vrf attach <VRF-NAME> command assigns an IP tunnel to a VRF. By default, all tunnels are automatically assigned to the default VRF when they are created. Public vrf attach (interface tunnel) 1705 The no form of this command assigns a tunnel to the default VRF (default). Specifies the VRF name to which to assign the tunnel. <VRF-NAME>. Syntax: no vrf attach <VRF-NAME>.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip dhcp option IPv6-only-preferred command do?

The ip dhcp option IPv6-only-preferred command enables the IPv6-only-preferred mode on the VLAN interface, and is also supported on the OOBM interface. Once enabled, the DHCP client prioritizes a IPv6 address over an IPv4 address. To obtain a DHCPv4 Public ip dhcp option IPv6-only-preferred 1724 address, the IPv6-only-preferred command must be disabled, and the DHCPv4 client must be restarted. This command is disabled by default. NOTE Ensure the server supports the IPv6-only-preferred mode option, as the command will not work unless supported by the server. The no form of this command disables IPv6-only-preferred mode. NOTE The IPv6-only-preferred mode command must be configured prior to the ip dhcp command, otherwise it will not take effect. Syntax: no ip dhcp option IPv6-only-preferred.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 address <global-unicast-address> command do?

The IPv6 address <global-unicast-address> command sets a global unicast address on the interface. The no form of this command removes the global unicast address on the interface. NOTE This command automatically creates an IPv6 link-local address on the interface. However, it does not add the IPv6 address link-local command to the running configuration. If you remove the IPv6 address, the link-local address is also removed. To maintain the link-local address, you must manually execute the IPv6 address link-local command. Syntax: no IPv6 address <global-unicast-address>.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 nd ra min-interval command do?

The IPv6 nd ra min-interval command configures the minimum interval between transmissions of IPv6 RAs on the interface. The interval between RA transmissions on an interface is a random value that changes every time an RA is sent. The interval is calculated to be a value between the current max-interval and min-interval settings. The no form of this command returns the setting to its default, provided the default value is less than the current max-interval setting. Specifies a minimum advertisement time in seconds. Range: 3 -1350. Default: 200 seconds. <TIME>. Syntax: IPv6 nd ra min-interval <TIME>.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the show IPv6 nd ra dns server command do?

The show IPv6 nd ra dns server command displays DNS server information on all interfaces. Shows the output from the VSX peer switch. If the switches do not have the VSX configuration or the ISL is down, the output f rom the VSX peer switch is not displayed. This parameter is avai lable on switches that support VSX. On the HPE Aruba Networking 6400 Switch Series, interface identification differs. switch(config)# interface 1/1/1 switch(config-if)# IPv6 nd ra dns server 2001: : 1 switch# show IPv6 nd ra dns server Recursive DNS Server List on: 1 Suppress DNS Server List: Yes DNS Server 1: 2001: : 1 lifetime 1800 NOTE For more information on features that use this command, use the search function within the AOS-CX doc portal. Syntax: show IPv6 nd ra dns server [vsx-peer].

command: show IPv6 nd ra dns server
syntax: show IPv6 nd ra dns server [vsx-peer]

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 source-binding <VLAN-ID> command do?

The IPv6 source-binding <VLAN-ID> command adds static IPv6 client source binding information to the switch IPv6 binding database. Although DHCPv6 snooping is often used to dynamically populate the binding database, this command is available for manually adding entries to the switch IPv6 binding database. NOTE Statically configured IPv6 binding information supersedes any dynamically collected binding information for the same client. The no form of this command removes the specified binding that was statically configured with the IPv6 source-binding command. The no form has no effect on bindings that were dynamically configured with DHCPv6 snooping. Specifies the ID of an existing VLAN on which the client is conn ected. Range: 1 to 4094. <VLAN-ID> <IPv6-ADDR> <MAC-ADDR> <IFNAME>. Syntax: no IPv6 source-binding <VLAN-ID>.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 source-lockdown hardware retry command do?

The IPv6 source-lockdown hardware retry command retries the IPv6 source lockdown hardware programming for a client identified by VLAN and IPv6 address. Specifies the ID of an existing VLAN on which the client is conn ected. Range: 1 to 4094. <VLAN-ID> <IPv6-ADDR> Shows all IPv6 static source binding information irrespective of source lockdown configuration. Shows the output from the VSX peer switch. If the switches do not have the VSX configuration or the ISL is down, the output f rom the VSX peer switch is not displayed. This parameter is avai lable on switches that support VSX. On the HPE Aruba Networking 6400 Switch Series, interface identification differs. Syntax: IPv6 source-lockdown hardware retry <VLAN-ID>.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip irdp holdtime <TIME> command do?

The ip irdp holdtime <TIME> command specifies the maximum amount of time the host will consider an advertisement to be valid until a newer advertisement arrives. When a new advertisement arrives, hold time is reset. Hold time must be greater than or equal to the maximum advertisement interval. Therefore, if the hold time for an advertisement expires, the host can reasonably conclude that the router interface that sent the advertisement is no longer available. The default hold time is three times the maximum advertisement interval. The no form of this command removes the specified maximum amount of time the host will consider an advertisement to be valid until a newer advertisement arrives and update it to the default value. Specifies the lifetime of router advertisements sent from this in terface. Range: 4 to 9000 seconds. Syntax: no ip irdp holdtime <TIME>.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip irdp maxadvertinterval <TIME> command do?

The ip irdp maxadvertinterval <TIME> command specifies the maximum router advertisement interval. Public ip irdp maxadvertinterval 1784 The no form of this command removes the specified maximum router advertisement interval and reverts to the default value. Specifies the maximum time allowed between the sending of u nsolicited router advertisements. Range: 4 to 1800 seconds. De fault: 600 seconds. <TIME>. Syntax: no ip irdp maxadvertinterval <TIME>.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip irdp minadvertinterval <TIME> command do?

The ip irdp minadvertinterval <TIME> command specifies the minimum amount of time the switch waits between sending router advertisements. By default, this value is automatically set by the switch to be 75% of the value configured for maximum router advertisement interval. Use this command to override the automatically configured value. The no form of this command removes the specified minimum amount of time the switch waits between sending router advertisements and reverts to the default value. Specifies the minimum time allowed between the sending of u nsolicited router advertisements. Range: 3 to 1800 seconds. De fault: 450 seconds (75% of the default value for maximum route r advertisement interval). <TIME>. Syntax: no ip irdp minadvertinterval <TIME>.

The router bgp vrf command this command configures VRF for the BGP instance. The no form of this command removes the configuration. Specifies a 4-byte AS number in the range 1-4294967295 i n integer format or from 0.1-65535.65535 in dotted format. String VRF name for the VRF. AS-NUMBER <VRF-NAME> Shows the BGP-VPN per VRF routes with additional route information like RD and extended community route targets. Displays the BGP neighbor information for the specified VRF. NOTE By default the default_vrf BGP instance information is displayed if the VRF is not specified. Syntax: router bgp <AS-NUMBER> [vrf <VRF-NAME>].

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the interface persona lag <NAME> command do?

The interface persona lag <NAME> command creates a persona LAG, a template for LAG (Link Aggregation Group) interfaces. A persona LAG defines configuration options that can be applied to multiple LAGs for consistency and simplified management. Only LAG interfaces can reference persona LAG templates. The persona LAG name cannot use reserved interface names or formats, such as vlan, lag, or loopback. Configurations defined in a persona LAG are valid only for LAG interfaces. The no form of this command deletes the persona LAG interface. Specifies the name of the persona LAG. <NAME>. Syntax: no interface persona lag <NAME>.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip address <IPv4-ADDR>/<MASK> [secondary] command do?

The ip address <IPv4-ADDR>/<MASK> [secondary] command sets an IPv4 address and subnet mask to a LAG interface. One primary and up to 31 secondary address can be configured per interface. The no form of this command removes the IPv4 address from the interface. Specifies an IP address in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), where x is a de cimal number from 0 to 255. You can remove leading zeros. For example, the address 192.169.005.100 becomes 192.168.5.1 00. Specifies the number of bits in the address mask in CIDR format (x), where x is a decimal number from 0 to 32. Specifies a secondary IP address. <IPv4-ADDR> <MASK> secondary. Syntax: no ip address <IPv4-ADDR>/<MASK> [secondary].

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the aaa authentication login privilege-elevation group administrators command do?

The aaa authentication login privilege-elevation group administrators command enables/disables privilege elevation for the users in an administrators group. When enabled, an administrative user is provided lower privilege access upon successful switch login. The user must execute an enable command and re-authenticate to elevate their privilege to administrator (the administrator level Public aaa authentication login privilege-elevation 1987 access with ‘#’ prompt). Executing the disable command revokes an administrator privilege and returns the user to the lower privilege access level (the operator level access ‘>’ prompt). The no form of this command disables privilege elevation for the administrator user group. Syntax: no aaa authentication login privilege-elevation group administrators.

command: aaa authentication login privilege-elevation group administrators

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the aaa authorization commands <CONNECTION-TYPE> group <GROUP-LIST> command do?

The aaa authorization commands <CONNECTION-TYPE> group <GROUP-LIST> command defines authorization as being basic local RBAC (specified as none), or as full-fledged local RBAC specified as local (the default), or as remote TACACS+ (specified with group <GROUP-LIST>). Each available connection type (channel) can be configured individually. All server groups named in the command, must exist. This command can be issued multiple times, once for each connection type. The no form of this command unconfigures authorization for the specified connection type, reverting to the default of local. NOTE Although only TACACS+ servers are supported for remote authorization, local authorization (basic or full-fledged) can be used with remote RADIUS authentication. Syntax: no aaa authorization commands <CONNECTION-TYPE> group <GROUP-LIST>.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the clear mdns-sd statistics command do?

The clear mdns-sd statistics command clears all mDNS gateway statistics. Adds a filter rule to the service profile. The sequence number configured determines the priority with which the rule is matched. Lower the sequence number, higher is the priority. Following are the filter match parameters: • Service-name: mDNS packets are matched against the service IDs configured under the service name. • Service-instance-name: mDNS packets are matched against the service instance name present in the mDNS packets. When no match criteria is specified in the rule, then the rule can be matched against any mDNS packet. Once the match is found then either the packet can be permitted or denied based on the action specified in the rule. The no form of this command deletes the filter configured in the service profile. Syntax: clear mdns-sd statistics.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the show mdns-sd statistics profile command do?

The show mdns-sd statistics profile command displays the number of packets permitted or denied by various filter rules in a profile. Specifies the profile name. Maximum 32 characters. <PROFILE-NAME> Shows whether mDNS gateway is enabled globally and at the VLAN interface level. It also shows the profile applied on various VLAN interfaces. Shows the configuration of profiles for an interface. Specifies the interface name. <INTERFACE-NAME> Shows the configuration of all or a specific profile. Specifies the profile name. Maximum 32 characters. <PROFILE-NAME> Shows the running configuration of all or a specific mDNS service. Specifies the service name. Maximum 32 characters. Public show running-config mdns-sd service 2112. Syntax: show mdns-sd statistics profile <PROFILE-NAME>.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 mld snooping apply access-list <ACL-NAME> command do?

The IPv6 mld snooping apply access-list <ACL-NAME> command configures the ACL on a particular interface to filter the MLD join or leave packets based on rules set in the particular ACL name. The no form of this command disables the rules set for the ACL. NOTE This configuration will override the ACL associated with IGMP snooping on the corresponding L2 VLAN. Associates an ACL with the IGMP. Public IPv6 mld snooping apply access-list 2180 Specifies the name of the ACL. <ACL-NAME> NOTE If the access list is configured for both L2 VLA N and L3 VLAN, the L3 VLAN configuration will be applied. Syntax: no IPv6 mld snooping apply access-list <ACL-NAME>.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 mld snooping all-vlans command do?

The IPv6 mld snooping all-vlans command enable MLD snooping on all VLANs. This configuration setting enables MLD snooping on all existing VLANs, as well as on any VLANs that are created after the IPv6 mld snooping all-vlans command is issued. When MLD snooping is enabled globally, MLD snooping can not be configured on individual VLANs within the VLAN context of the command-line interface. The no form of this command disables global MLD snooping, allows MLD snooping to remain enabled at the VLAN level, and allows the configuration of VLAN-level MLD snooping settings. Syntax: no IPv6 mld snooping all-vlans.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 mld snooping [auto vlan <VLAN-LIST>] command do?

The IPv6 mld snooping [auto vlan <VLAN-LIST>] command this command configures the given ports in auto mode, which is the default port mode. The no form of this command disables auto ports. Required: Specifies a list of VLANs on which the port should be configured as an auto port. Specifies the number of a single VL AN or a series of numbers for a range of VLANs, separated by c ommas (10, 20, 30, 40), dashes (10-40), or both (10-40, 60). On the HPE Aruba Networking 6400 Switch Series, interface identification differs. Syntax: no IPv6 mld snooping [auto vlan <VLAN-LIST>].

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 mld snooping [blocked vlan <VLAN-LIST>] command do?

The IPv6 mld snooping [blocked vlan <VLAN-LIST>] command by default ports are configured in auto mode. This command configures the given ports in blocked mode. The no form of this command removes blocked ports. Required: Specifies a list of VLANs on which the port should be configured as a blocked port. Specifies the number of a single V LAN or a series of numbers for a range of VLANs, separated by commas (10, 20, 30, 40), dashes (10-40), or both (10-40, 6 0). On the HPE Aruba Networking 6400 Switch Series, interface identification differs. Syntax: IPv6 mld snooping fastlearn <port-list>.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 mld snooping [fastleave vlan <VLAN-LIST>] command do?

The IPv6 mld snooping [fastleave vlan <VLAN-LIST>] command configures the specified ports as fastleave ports. Enables the switch to immediately remove an interface from the bridge table upon receiving the leave group message. Public IPv6 mld snooping fastleave vlan 2187 The no form of this command disables fastleave configuration on the ports. Required: Specifies a list of VLANs on which the port should be configured as a fastleave port. Specifies the number of a single VLAN or a series of numbers for a range of VLANs, separated by commas (10, 20, 30, 40), dashes (10-40), or both (10-40, 60). MLD fastleave is configured for ports on a per-VLAN basis. Syntax: no IPv6 mld snooping [fastleave vlan <VLAN-LIST>].

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 mld snooping [forced-fastleave <VLAN-LIST>] command do?

The IPv6 mld snooping [forced-fastleave <VLAN-LIST>] command configures the given ports in forced fastleave mode. The no form of this command disables forced fastleave configuration on the ports. Required: Specifies a list of VLANs on which the port should be configured as a forced fastleave port. Specifies the number of a single VLAN or a series of numbers for a range of VLANs, sepa Public IPv6 mld snooping forced fastleave vlan 2190 rated by commas (10, 20, 30, 40), dashes (10-40), or both (1 0-40, 60). With forced fastleave enabled, MLD speeds up the process of blocking unnecessary multicast traffic to a switch port that is connected to multiple end nodes. Syntax: no IPv6 mld snooping [forced-fastleave <VLAN-LIST>].

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 mld snooping vxlan replicate-all command do?

The IPv6 mld snooping vxlan replicate-all command this command enables AOS-CX leaf devices (access-layer switches that connect directly to servers, storage, and other endpoints) to unconditionally forward multicast traffic to all VTEPs that do not support the exchange of MLD reports over the fabric. By default, when MLD snooping is enabled in AOS-CX, multicast traffic is selectively forwarded only to VTEPs connected to have interested hosts. This is achieved by exhanging MLD reports over VXLAN tunnels where each VTEP processes the remote reports and uses the membership information to forward multicast traffic efficiently, conserving fabric bandwidth. Syntax: no IPv6 mld snooping vxlan replicate-all.

syntax: no IPv6 mld snooping vxlan replicate-all

The IPv6 mld version <VERSION> command this command configures MLD version. The no form of the command configures the default MLD version of 2. Required: 1-2, configures MLD version. <VERSION> This command configures MLD strict version. Packets that do not match the configured version will be dropped. messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the show capacities mpls command do?

The show capacities mpls command for capacities command, shows the maximum number of label endpoints, label switch entries, and service label entries that can be configured on the device. For capacities-status command, shows the total number of label endpoints, label switch entries, and service label entries that are currently configured on the device. Shows the MPLS forwarding table. Shows the range of MPLS labels allocated for use in static LSP bindings and the range of labels currently used by static LSP bindings. Shows information about all MPLS LDP bindings. Shows information about discovered LDP peers. <IP-ADDR> Specifies the peer MPLS LDP router ID in x.x.x.x format, where x is a decimal value from 0 to 255. Public show mpls ldp discovery 2293 Shows graceful restart parameters and status. Syntax: show capacities mpls.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the show ip msdp count command do?

The show ip msdp count command shows MSDP Peer (S, G) learnt count for a given VRF. If no options are specified, it shows information for the default VRF. Shows MSDP (S, G) entries count for all VRFs. Optional. Shows MSDP (S, G) entries count for a particular VRF. If the <VR F-NAME> is not specified, it shows information for the default VRF. Optional. Shows MSDP Peer information for the given VRF. Optionally, you can show specific information by VRF. Shows MSDP peer information for all VRFs. Optional. Shows MSDP peer information for a particular VRF. If the <VRF -NAME> is not specified, it shows information for the default VRF. Optional. Shows MSDP Peer information for specified Peer IP. Format: A. B. C. D. Optional. Shows MSDP Peer SA-Cache information for the given VRF. Syntax: show ip msdp count [all-vrfs | vrf <VRF-NAME>].

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip pim-sparse datapath-auto-include command do?

The ip pim-sparse datapath-auto-include command enables the router to forward multicast data received on the VXLAN L3VNI fabric to this interface, regardless of whether a multicast join was received on this interface or not. This allows the interface to be in the same multicast data path state on both the VSX peers. This command must be enabled on the VSX VXLAN routers to support multicast receivers over ROP or P2P SVI extensions. This command can be enabled on a transit L3 peering between VSX peers. This command is optional when the uplink is an MCLAGsVI. The no form of the command disables forwarding of multicast data on the interface. NOTE • An IP address must be configured on the interface and pim-sparse must be enabled. • This command must be enabled only on one interface per VRF. Syntax: no ip pim-sparse datapath-auto-include.

The IPv6 pim6-sparse datapath-auto-include command enables the router to forward multicast data received on the VXLAN L3VNI fabric to this interface, regardless of whether a multicast join was received on this interface or not. This command must be enabled on the VSX VXLAN routers to support multicast receivers over ROP or P2P SVI extensions. This command can be enabled on a transit L3 peering between VSX peers. This command should not be enabled when the uplink is an MCLAGsVI. The no form of the command disables forwarding of multicast data on the interface. NOTE • An IP address must be configured on the interface and pim-sparse must be enabled. • This command must be enabled only on one interface per VRF. messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the show nd-snooping statistics command do?

The show nd-snooping statistics command shows the global ND snooping statistics. Shows the output from the VSX peer switch. If the switches do not have the VSX configuration or the ISL is down, the output f rom the VSX peer switch is not displayed. This parameter is avai lable on switches that support VSX. Syntax: show nd-snooping statistics [vsx-peer].

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ntp enable command do?

The ntp enable command enables the NTP client on the switch to automatically adjust the local time and date on the switch. The NTP client is disabled by default. The no form of this command disables the NTP client. Syntax: no ntp enable.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ntp trusted-key <KEY-ID> command do?

The ntp trusted-key <KEY-ID> command sets a key as trusted. When NTP authentication is enabled, the switch only synchronizes with time servers that transmit packets containing a trusted key. The no form of this command removes the trusted designation from a key. Specifies the identification number of the key to set as trusted. Range: 1 to 65534. Public ntp trusted-key 2550. Syntax: no ntp trusted-key <KEY-ID>.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 ospfv3 {neighbor <neighbor-IPv6-address>} command do?

The IPv6 ospfv3 {neighbor <neighbor-IPv6-address>} command configures a static OSPFv3 neighbor on the interface for point-to-multipoint networks. Static neighbors are required on non-broadcast networks where automatic neighbor discovery using multicast hello packets is not possible. The neighbor IPv6 address must be the link-local IPv6 address of the neighboring router on the same link. This command is valid only on interfaces configured with the point-to-multipoint network type. It is required for non-broadcast point-to-multipoint networks. The no version of this command removes the static neighbor configuration from the interface. neighbor <neighbor-IPv6- address> Static neighbor IPv6 link-local address. Public IPv6 ospfv3 neighbor 2714. Syntax: no IPv6 ospfv3 {neighbor <neighbor-IPv6-address>}.

document_title: Command Line Interface Reference Guide For 6300 And 6400 Switch Series
source_file: 6300_6400/10_17_1000/Command-line interface Reference Guide For 6300 and 6400 Switch Series.md

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 ospfv3 <PROCESS-ID> area <AREA-ID> command do?

The IPv6 ospfv3 <PROCESS-ID> area <AREA-ID> command runs the OSPFv3 protocol on the interface for the area specified. To move an interface to a new area, unmap the existing area and then associate a new area with the interface. The no form of this command disables OSPF on the interface and removes the interface from the area. Interfaces which have an IP address configured on the network or in a subset of the network, stop participating in the OSPF protocol Specifies the area ID is one of the following formats. • Area ID in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), where x is a decimal num ber from 0 to 255. • Area ID as a decimal value. Range: 0-4294967295. Specifies the OSPFv3 process ID. Range: 1 to 65535. <AREA-ID> <PROCESS-ID> Configures null authentication on an interface which disables IPsec authentication. Configures IPSec AH authentication. Syntax: IPv6 ospfv3 authentication null.

document_title: Command Line Interface Reference Guide For 6300 And 6400 Switch Series
source_file: 6300_6400/10_17_1000/Command-line interface Reference Guide For 6300 and 6400 Switch Series.md

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 ospfv3 encryption null command do?

The IPv6 ospfv3 encryption null command configures NULL ESP on an interface which disables IPsec ESP. Sets the time interval between OSPFv3 hello packets for the OSPFv3 interface. The no form of this command sets the time interval between OSPFv3 hello packets to the default for the OSPFv3 interface of 10 seconds. Specifies the time interval between hello packets, in seconds. R ange: 1 to 65535. Default: 10. <INTERVAL> Configures the network type for the interface. By default, the network type is broadcast. The no form of this command sets the network type for the interface to the system default of broadcast network. Specifies the OSPFv3 network type as a broadcast multiaccess network. Specifies the OSPFv3 network type as a point-to-point netw ork. Syntax: IPv6 ospfv3 encryption null.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 pim6-dense hello-delay command do?

The IPv6 pim6-dense hello-delay command configures the maximum time in seconds before the router actually transmits the initial PIM hello message on the current interface. The no form of this command removes currently configured value and sets to the default of 5 seconds. Specifies the hello-delay in seconds, which is the maximum ti me before a triggered PIM Hello message is transmitted on this interface. Default: 5 seconds. Range: 0-5. <DELAY-VALUE> Configures the frequency at which the router transmits PIM hello messages on the current interface. The no form of this command removes the currently configured value and sets to the default of 30 seconds. Public IPv6 pim6-dense hello-interval 2871 Specifies the frequency at which PIM Hello messages are trans mitted on this interface. Default: 30 seconds. Range: 5-300. Syntax: IPv6 pim6-dense hello-delay <DELAY-VALUE>.

document_title: Command Line Interface Reference Guide For 6300 And 6400 Switch Series
source_file: 6300_6400/10_17_1000/Command-line interface Reference Guide For 6300 and 6400 Switch Series.md

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 pim6-dense propagation-delay command do?

The IPv6 pim6-dense propagation-delay command configures the propagation delay that gets inserted into the LAN prune delay field of a LAN Prune Delay option. The no form of this command removes currently configured value and sets to the default of 500 ms. Specifies the propagation delay value in ms. Default: 500 ms. R ange: 250-2000. <DELAY-VALUE> Configures the multicast datagram time-to-live (router hop-count) threshold for the interface. Any IP multicast datagrams or state-refresh packets with a TTL less than this threshold will not be forwarded out the interface. The no form of this command removes the currently configured value and sets to the default of 3 attempts. Public IPv6 pim6-dense ttl-threshold 2881 Specifies the time-to-live threshold. Default: 3 attempts. Ran ge: 0-255. Syntax: IPv6 pim6-dense propagation-delay <DELAY-VALUE>.

source_file: 6300_6400/10_17_1000/Command-line interface Reference Guide For 6300 and 6400 Switch Series.md

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the show IPv6 pim6 rpf (PIM-DM) command do?

The show IPv6 pim6 rpf (PIM-DM) command displays PIM RPF details for the specified source or RP address in the given VRF and shows the nexthop and interface through which the shortest path to the source is available. It also displays if a PIM neighbor is present on the nexthop. Optionally, you can specify display information by VRF. If no options are specified, it shows information for the default VRF. <IP-ADDRESS> all-vrfs vrf <VRF-NAME> vsx-peer Optional. Specifies an IP address in IPv6 format (xxxx: xxxx: xxx x: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx), where x is a hexadecimal number f rom 0 to F. Shows PIM RPF details for the given IP address. Optional. Shows PIM interface information for all VRFs. Optional. Shows PIM interface information for a particular VRF. I f the <VRF-NAME> is not specified, it shows the default VRF i nformation. Syntax: show IPv6 pim6 rpf [<IP-ADDRESS>][all-vrfs | vrf <VRF-NAME>] [vsx-peer].

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 mroute (router pim6) command do?

The IPv6 mroute (router pim6) command configures multicast reverse path (RPF) forwarding static routes. This command is an alias of the rpf- override command. The no form of this command removes the mroute configuration. Specifies the multicast source address in IPv6 format (xxxx: xx xx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx), where x is a hexadecima l number from 0 to F. Specifies the number of bits in the address mask in CIDR format (x), where x is a decimal number from 0 to 128. Specifies the RPF address in IPv6 format (xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxx x: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx), where x is a hexadecimal number fro m 0 to F. Specifies the RPF interface name. <SRC-ADDR> <SRC-MASK> <RPF-ADDR> <INTERFACE-NAME>. Syntax: IPv6 mroute <SRC-ADDR/SRC-MASK>.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the crypto pki application <APP-NAME> certificate <CERT-NAME> command do?

The crypto pki application <APP-NAME> certificate <CERT-NAME> command configures an application to associate with a user-configured certificate. By default, all applications are associated with the default certificate "local-cert". Note that applications "captive-portal" and "hsc" are only available on certain platforms. The certificate associated with EST client is global to all EST profiles. Public PKI commands 3184 Among the available applications, only dot1x-supplicant, est-client, and radsec-client are allowed to associate with device-identity certificate. Also note that device-identity certificate on a switch using a TPM of legacy version 1.2 only works with TLS version 1.2. 1. Syntax: no crypto pki application <APP-NAME> certificate <CERT-NAME>.
