document_title: Ip Services Guide (6300, 6400)

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, how do you configure IRDP?

To configure IRDP, follow the documented procedure: 1. Enable IRDP on an interface with the command ip irdp 2. Set the maximum hold time with the command ip irdp holdtime 3. Set the maximum router advertisement interval with the command ip irdp maxadvertinterv.

document_title: Ip Services Guide (6300, 6400)

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the diag-dump irdp basic command do?
The diag-dump irdp basic command displays diagnostic information for IRDP. Syntax: diag-dump irdp basic.

document_title: Ip Services Guide (6300, 6400)

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what is the syntax of the diag-dump irdp basic command?
The syntax of the diag-dump irdp basic command is: diag-dump irdp basic.

document_title: Ip Services Guide (6300, 6400)

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip irdp command do?

The ip irdp command enables IRDP on an interface and specifies the packet type that is used to send advertisements. By default, the packet type is set to multicast. IRDP is only supported on layer 3 interfaces. The no form of this command disables IRDP on an interface. Advertisements are sent as broadcast packets to IP address 25 5.255.255.255. Advertisements are sent as multicast packets to the multicast g roup with IP address 24.0.0.1. Default. On the HPE Aruba Networking 6400 Switch Series, interface identification differs. Enabling IRDP on interface 1/1/1 with packet type set to the default value (multicast). switch(config)# interface 1/1/1 switch(config-if)# ip irdp Enabling IRDP on interface 1/1/1 with packet type set to broadcast. Syntax: ip irdp [broadcast | multicast].

document_title: Ip Services Guide (6300, 6400)

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what is the syntax of the ip irdp command?
The syntax of the ip irdp command is: ip irdp [broadcast | multicast]

document_title: Ip Services Guide (6300, 6400)

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 address autoconfig command do?

The IPv6 address autoconfig command enables the interface to automatically obtain an IPv6 address using router advertisement information and the EUI-64 identifier. The no form of this command disables address auto-configuration. NOTE • A maximum of 15 autoconfigured addresses are supported. • This command automatically creates an IPv6 link-local address on the interface. However, it does not add the IPv6 address link-loca l command to the running configuration. If you remove the IPv6 address, the link-local address is also removed. To maintain the link-local address, you must manually execute the IPv6 address link-local command. Syntax: IPv6 address autoconfig.

document_title: Ip Services Guide (6300, 6400)

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what is the syntax of the IPv6 address autoconfig command?
The syntax of the IPv6 address autoconfig command is: IPv6 address autoconfig.

document_title: Ip Services Guide (6300, 6400)

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 address link-local command do?

The IPv6 address link-local command enables IPv6 on the current interface. If no address is specified, an IPv6 link-local address is auto-generated for the interface. If an address is specified, auto-configuration is disabled and the specified address/mask is assigned to the interface. Public IPv6 address link-local 39 To disable IPv6 link-local on the interface, remove IPv6 address link-local, IPv6 address <global-IPv6- address>, and IPv6 address autoconfig from the interface. NOTE This feature is not available on the management VRF. Specifies the IP address in IPv6 format (xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx), where x is a hexadecimal number from 0 to F. You can use two colons (: :) to represent consecutive zero s (but only once), remove leading zeros, and collapse a hextet o f four zeros to a single 0. Syntax: IPv6 address link-local [<IPv6-ADDR>/<MASK>].

document_title: Ip Services Guide (6300, 6400)

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what is the syntax of the IPv6 address link-local command?
The syntax of the IPv6 address link-local command is: IPv6 address link-local [<IPv6-ADDR>/<MASK>]

document_title: Ip Services Guide (6300, 6400)

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 nd cache-limit command do?

The IPv6 nd cache-limit command configures the limit on the number of neighbor entries in the ND cache. The no form of this command sets the cache limit to the default value. Specifies the neighbor cache entries limit. Range: 1-131072. Default: 131072. <CACHELIMIT>. Syntax: IPv6 nd cache-limit <CACHELIMIT>.

document_title: Ip Services Guide (6300, 6400)

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what is the syntax of the IPv6 nd cache-limit command?
The syntax of the IPv6 nd cache-limit command is: IPv6 nd cache-limit <CACHELIMIT>

document_title: Ip Services Guide (6300, 6400)

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 nd dad attempts command do?

The IPv6 nd dad attempts command configures the number of neighbor solicitations to be sent when performing duplicate address detection (DAD) for a unicast address configured on an interface. If the active gateway is configured with the same IP as an SVI IP, then IPv6 DAD cannot be configured. The no form of this command sets the number of attempts to the default value. dad attempts <NUM-ATTEMPTS> Specifies the number of neighbor solicitations to send. Range: 0 -15. Default: 1. Syntax: IPv6 nd dad attempts <NUM-ATTEMPTS>.

document_title: Ip Services Guide (6300, 6400)

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what is the syntax of the IPv6 nd dad attempts command?
The syntax of the IPv6 nd dad attempts command is: IPv6 nd dad attempts <NUM-ATTEMPTS>

document_title: Ip Services Guide (6300, 6400)

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 nd hop-limit command do?

The IPv6 nd hop-limit command configures the hop limit to be sent in RAs. The no form of this command resets the hop limit to 0. This reset eliminates the hop limit from the RAs that originate on the interface, so the host determines the hop limit. hop-limit <HOPLIMIT> Specifies the hop limit. Range: 0-255. Default: 64. Syntax: IPv6 nd hop-limit <HOPLIMIT>.

document_title: Ip Services Guide (6300, 6400)

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 nd mtu command do?

The IPv6 nd mtu command configures the MTU size to be sent in the RA messages. The no form of this command sets hop limit to the default value. Specifies the MTU size. Range: 1280-65535 bytes. Default: 1 500 bytes. <MTU-VALUE>. Syntax: IPv6 nd mtu <MTU-VALUE>.

document_title: Ip Services Guide (6300, 6400)

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what is the syntax of the IPv6 nd mtu command?
The syntax of the IPv6 nd mtu command is: IPv6 nd mtu <MTU-VALUE>

document_title: Ip Services Guide (6300, 6400)

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 nd ns-interval command do?

The IPv6 nd ns-interval command configures the ND time in milliseconds between DAD neighbor solicitations sent for an unresolved destination. Increase the ns-interval time if the network is slow or if there are persistent retry failures. If the active gateway is configured with the same IP as an SVI IP, then IPv6 DAD cannot be configured The no form of this command sets the ns-interval to the default value. Specifies the neighbor solicitation interval. Range: 1000-3600 000 milliseconds. Default: 1000 milliseconds. <TIME>. Syntax: IPv6 nd ns-interval <TIME>.

document_title: Ip Services Guide (6300, 6400)

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what is the syntax of the IPv6 nd ns-interval command?
The syntax of the IPv6 nd ns-interval command is: IPv6 nd ns-interval <TIME>

document_title: Ip Services Guide (6300, 6400)

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 nd prefix command do?

The IPv6 nd prefix command specifies prefixes for the routing switch to include in RAs transmitted on the interface. IPv6 hosts use the prefixes in RAs to autoconfigure themselves with global unicast addresses. The autoconfigured address of a host is composed of the advertised prefix and the interface identifier in the current link-local address of the host. By default, advertise, autoconfig, and onlink are set. The no form of this command removes the configuration on the interface. Public IPv6 nd prefix 46 Specifies the IPv6 prefix to advertise in RA. Format: X: X: : X: X/M <IPv6-ADDR>/<PREFIX-LEN> default Specifies apply configuration to all on-link prefixes that are n ot individually set by the IPv6 ra prefix <IPv6-ADDR>/<PRE FIX-LEN> command. Syntax: IPv6 nd prefix <IPv6-ADDR>/<PREFIX-LEN>.

document_title: Ip Services Guide (6300, 6400)

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what is the syntax of the IPv6 nd prefix command?
The syntax of the IPv6 nd prefix command is: IPv6 nd prefix <IPv6-ADDR>/<PREFIX-LEN>

document_title: Ip Services Guide (6300, 6400)

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 nd ra dns search-list command do?

The IPv6 nd ra dns search-list command configures the DNS Search List (DNSSL) to include in Router Advertisements (RAs) transmitted on the interface. The no form of this command removes the DNS Search List from the RAs transmitted on the interface. Specifies the domain names for DNS queries. Public IPv6 nd ra dns search-list 48 Specifies lifetime in seconds. Range: 4-4294967295 seconds. Default: 1800 seconds. • DNSSL contains the domain names of DNS suffixes or IPv6 hosts to append to short, unqualified domain names for DNS queries. • Multiple DNS domain names can be added to the DNSSL by using the command repeatedly. • A maximum of eight server addresses are allowed. Syntax: IPv6 nd ra dns search-list <DOMAIN-NAME> [lifetime <TIME>].

document_title: Ip Services Guide (6300, 6400)

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what is the syntax of the IPv6 nd ra dns search-list command?
The syntax of the IPv6 nd ra dns search-list command is: IPv6 nd ra dns search-list <DOMAIN-NAME> [lifetime <TIME>]

document_title: Ip Services Guide (6300, 6400)

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 nd ra lifetime command do?

The IPv6 nd ra lifetime command configures the lifetime, in seconds, for the routing switch to be used as a default router by hosts on the current interface. The no form of this command sets lifetime to the default of 1800 seconds. Specifies lifetime in seconds of a default router. A setting of 0 fo r default router lifetime in an RA indicates that the routing switc h is not a default router on the interface. Range: 0-9000 seco nds. Default: 1800 seconds. <TIME>. Syntax: IPv6 nd ra lifetime <TIME>.

document_title: Ip Services Guide (6300, 6400)

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what is the syntax of the IPv6 nd ra lifetime command?
The syntax of the IPv6 nd ra lifetime command is: IPv6 nd ra lifetime <TIME>

document_title: Ip Services Guide (6300, 6400)

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 nd ra managed-config-flag command do?

The IPv6 nd ra managed-config-flag command controls the M fLAGsetting in RAs the router transmits on the current interface. Enable the M flag to indicate that hosts can obtain IP address through DHCPv6. The M flag is disabled by default. The no form of this command turns off (disables) the M flag. Syntax: IPv6 nd ra managed-config-flag.

document_title: Ip Services Guide (6300, 6400)

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what is the syntax of the IPv6 nd ra managed-config-flag command?
The syntax of the IPv6 nd ra managed-config-flag command is: IPv6 nd ra managed-config-flag.

document_title: Ip Services Guide (6300, 6400)

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 nd ra max-interval command do?

The IPv6 nd ra max-interval command configures the maximum interval between transmissions of IPv6 RAs on the interface. The interval between RA transmissions on an interface is a random value that changes every time an RA is sent. The interval is calculated to be a value between the current max-interval and min-interval settings. The no form of this command returns the setting to its default, provided the default value is less than the default lifetime value. Specifies the maximum advertisement time in seconds. Range: 4-1800. Default: 600 seconds. <TIME>. Syntax: IPv6 nd ra max-interval <TIME>.

document_title: Ip Services Guide (6300, 6400)

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what is the syntax of the IPv6 nd ra max-interval command?
The syntax of the IPv6 nd ra max-interval command is: IPv6 nd ra max-interval <TIME>

document_title: Ip Services Guide (6300, 6400)

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 nd ra other-config-flag command do?

The IPv6 nd ra other-config-flag command controls the O-bit in RAs the router transmits on the current interface; but is ignored unless the M-bit is disabled in RAs. Configure to set the O-bit in RA messages for host to obtain network parameters through DHCPv6. The other-config-flag is disabled by default. Public IPv6 nd ra other-config-flag 56 For more information on configuring the M-bit, see IPv6 nd ra managed-config-flag. The no form of this command turns off (disables) the setting for this command in RAs. Syntax: IPv6 nd ra other-config-flag.

document_title: Ip Services Guide (6300, 6400)

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what is the syntax of the IPv6 nd ra other-config-flag command?
The syntax of the IPv6 nd ra other-config-flag command is: IPv6 nd ra other-config-flag.

document_title: Ip Services Guide (6300, 6400)

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 nd ra reachable-time command do?

The IPv6 nd ra reachable-time command sets the amount of time that the interface considers a device to be reachable after receiving a reachability confirmation from the device. Public IPv6 nd ra reachable-time 57 The no form of this command sets the reachable time to the default value of 0. (no limit). Specifies the reachable time in milliseconds. Range: 1000-36 00000. Default: 0 (no limit). <TIME>. Syntax: IPv6 nd ra reachable-time <TIME>.

document_title: Ip Services Guide (6300, 6400)

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what is the syntax of the IPv6 nd ra reachable-time command?
The syntax of the IPv6 nd ra reachable-time command is: IPv6 nd ra reachable-time <TIME>

document_title: Ip Services Guide (6300, 6400)

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 nd ra retrans-timer command do?

The IPv6 nd ra retrans-timer command configures the period (retransmit timer) between ND solicitations sent by a host for an unresolved destination, or between DAD neighbor solicitation requests. By default, hosts on the interface use their own locally configured NS-interval settings instead of using the value received in the RAs. Increase this timer when neighbor solicitation retries or failures are occur, or in a "slow" (WAN) network. The no form of this command sets the value to the default of 0. Specifies the retransmit timer value in milliseconds. Range: 0 - 4294967295 milliseconds. Default: 0 (Use locally configured N S-interval). <TIME>. Syntax: IPv6 nd ra retrans-timer <TIME>.

document_title: Ip Services Guide (6300, 6400)

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what is the syntax of the IPv6 nd ra retrans-timer command?
The syntax of the IPv6 nd ra retrans-timer command is: IPv6 nd ra retrans-timer <TIME>

document_title: Ip Services Guide (6300, 6400)

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 nd router-preference command do?

The IPv6 nd router-preference command specifies the value that is set in the Default Router Preference (DRP) field of Router Advertisements (RAs) that the switch sends from an interface. An interface with a DRP value of high will be preferred by other devices on the network over interfaces with an RA value of medium or low. The no form of this command set the value to the default of medium. Sets DRP to high. Sets DRP to medium. Default. Sets DRP to low. On the HPE Aruba Networking 6400 Switch Series, interface identification differs. switch(config)# interface 1/1/1 Public IPv6 nd router-preference 61 switch(config-if)# IPv6 nd router-preference high. Syntax: IPv6 nd router-preference {high | medium | low}.

document_title: Ip Services Guide (6300, 6400)

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what is the syntax of the IPv6 nd router-preference command?
The syntax of the IPv6 nd router-preference command is: IPv6 nd router-preference {high | medium | low}.

document_title: Ip Services Guide (6300, 6400)

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 nd suppress-ra command do?

The IPv6 nd suppress-ra command configures suppression of IPv6 Router Advertisement transmissions on an interface. The no form of this command restores transmission of IPv6 Router Advertisement and options. suppress-ra [<SUPPRESS- OPTION>] Specifies suppressing RA transmissions. Entering suppress-ra without any options, suppresses all RA messages (default). Or you can enter one of the following options. dnssl mtu rdnss Specifies suppressing DNSSL options in RA messages. Specifies suppressing MTU options in RA messages. Specifies suppressing RDNSS options in RA messages. Public IPv6 nd suppress-ra 62. Syntax: IPv6 nd suppress-ra [<SUPPRESS-OPTION>].

document_title: Ip Services Guide (6300, 6400)

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what is the syntax of the IPv6 nd suppress-ra command?
The syntax of the IPv6 nd suppress-ra command is: IPv6 nd suppress-ra [<SUPPRESS-OPTION>]

document_title: Ip Services Guide (6300, 6400)

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the show IPv6 nd global traffic command do?

The show IPv6 nd global traffic command displays IPv6 Neighbor Discovery traffic details on a device. Shows the output from the VSX peer switch. If the switches do not have the VSX configuration or the ISL is down, the output f rom the VSX peer switch is not displayed. This parameter is avai lable on switches that support VSX. Public show IPv6 nd global traffic 63. Syntax: show IPv6 nd global traffic [vsx-peer].

document_title: Ip Services Guide (6300, 6400)

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what is the syntax of the show IPv6 nd global traffic command?
The syntax of the show IPv6 nd global traffic command is: show IPv6 nd global traffic [vsx-peer]

document_title: Ip Services Guide (6300, 6400)

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the show IPv6 nd interface prefix command do?

The show IPv6 nd interface prefix command shows IPv6 prefix information for all VRFs or a specific VRF. If no options are specified, shows information for the default VRF. Shows prefix information for all VRFs. vrf <VRF-NAME> Name of a VRF. vsx-peer. Syntax: show IPv6 nd interface prefix [all-vrfs | vrf <VRF-NAME>] [vsx-peer].

syntax: show IPv6 nd interface prefix [all-vrfs | vrf <VRF-NAME>] [vsx-peer]

document_title: Ip Services Guide (6300, 6400)

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what is the syntax of the show IPv6 nd interface prefix command?
The syntax of the show IPv6 nd interface prefix command is: show IPv6 nd interface prefix [all-vrfs | vrf <VRF-NAME>] [vsx-peer]

syntax: show IPv6 nd interface prefix [all-vrfs | vrf <VRF-NAME>] [vsx-peer]

document_title: Ip Services Guide (6300, 6400)

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the show IPv6 nd interface route command do?

The show IPv6 nd interface route command displays route information of all interfaces in the default VRF. Public show IPv6 nd interface route 72 Displays information about interfaces in all VRFs. Displays information about interfaces in a particular VRF. Or, if <VRF-NAME> is not specified, displays information for the de fault VRF. On the HPE Aruba Networking 6400 Switch Series, interface identification differs. Syntax: show IPv6 nd interface route [all-vrfs | vrf <VRF-NAME>].

syntax: show IPv6 nd interface route [all-vrfs | vrf <VRF-NAME>]

document_title: Ip Services Guide (6300, 6400)

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what is the syntax of the show IPv6 nd interface route command?
The syntax of the show IPv6 nd interface route command is: show IPv6 nd interface route [all-vrfs | vrf <VRF-NAME>]

syntax: show IPv6 nd interface route [all-vrfs | vrf <VRF-NAME>]

document_title: Ip Services Guide (6300, 6400)

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the guide say about Interface counters polling?

Interface counters polling: • Agent IP configuration for IPv4 and IPv6 • Header size configuration • Max datagram size configuration • Ingress sampling for all forwarded traffic (L2, L3) • Egress sampling for all forwarded traffic (L2, L3) • Enable/Disable sFlow per interface • Support for three remote collectors Public sFlow agent 77 • A collector can be defined on the default and non-default VRF • Sampling on Ethernet and LAG interfaces • High availability support (sampling continues to work after switch-over) • Source IP support (setting source IP for sFlow datagrams sent to a remote collector) Limitations • Sampling rate cannot be set per interface (global only) • sFlow is not configurable through SNMP.

document_title: Ip Services Guide (6300, 6400)

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the clear sflow statistics command do?

The clear sflow statistics command this command clears the sFlow sample statistics counter to 0 either globally or for a specific interface. Public sFlow agent commands 86 Specifies all interfaces on the switch. interface <INTERFACE-NAME> Specifies the name of an interface on the switch. Syntax: clear sflow statistics {global | interface <INTERFACE-NAME>}.

document_title: Ip Services Guide (6300, 6400)

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what is the syntax of the clear sflow statistics command?
The syntax of the clear sflow statistics command is: clear sflow statistics {global | interface <INTERFACE-NAME>}.

document_title: Ip Services Guide (6300, 6400)

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the no ip dhcp preferred-vlan vlan <VLAN-ID> [vrf <VRF-NAME>] command do?

The no ip dhcp preferred-vlan vlan <VLAN-ID> [vrf <VRF-NAME>] command specifies the preferred VLAN for DHCP client options, including DNS, NTP, and Default Gateway. The preferred VLAN is the VLAN used to select DHCP client options within a specified VRF when multiple VLANs have DHCP client enabled in the same VRF. If no VRF is specified, the configuration applies to the default VRF. In cases where the preferred VLAN is not explicitly configured, the lowest VLAN with DHCP client enabled will be automatically selected. The no form of the command removes the preferred VLAN configuration and restores the default behavior. Public ip dhcp preferred-vlan 111 VLAN ID to set as preferred VLAN. VLAN identifier range: 1 to 4094. Specifies the VRF in which the preferred VLANshould be confi gured.

document_title: Ip Services Guide (6300, 6400)

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the dhcp-relay hop-count-increment command do?

The dhcp-relay hop-count-increment command enables the DHCP relay hop count increment feature, which causes the DHCP relay agent to increment the hop count in all relayed DHCP packets. Hop count is enabled by default. The no form of this command disables the hop count increment feature. Syntax: dhcp-relay hop-count-increment.

document_title: Ip Services Guide (6300, 6400)

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what is the syntax of the dhcp-relay hop-count-increment command?
The syntax of the dhcp-relay hop-count-increment command is: dhcp-relay hop-count-increment.

document_title: Ip Services Guide (6300, 6400)

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the dhcp-relay l2vpn-clients command do?

The dhcp-relay l2vpn-clients command enables forwarding of packets from L2 VPN clients. Forwarding is enabled by default. Best practices is to disable this configuration on all the VXLAN tunnel endpoints (VTEPs), to avoid forwarding duplicate DHCP requests to the server. The no form of this command disables forwarding of packets from L2 VPN clients. Syntax: dhcp-relay l2vpn-clients.

document_title: Ip Services Guide (6300, 6400)

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what is the syntax of the dhcp-relay l2vpn-clients command?
The syntax of the dhcp-relay l2vpn-clients command is: dhcp-relay l2vpn-clients.

document_title: Ip Services Guide (6300, 6400)

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the dhcp-relay vsx active-active command do?

The dhcp-relay vsx active-active command enables DHCP relay active-active mode in VSX. When enabled, DHCP relay is active on both VSX Primary and Secondary peers. DHCP client requests are processed on the same VSX peer that receives them. A VSX peer that receives DHCP client request packets from its peer silently ignores them. By default, this feature is disabled, and DHCP request packets are forwarded to the configured helper addresses only by the VSX primary. The no form of this command disables DHCP relay active-active mode, and DHCP request packets are forwarded to configured helper addresses only by the VSX primary. Syntax: dhcp-relay vsx active-active.

document_title: Ip Services Guide (6300, 6400)

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what is the syntax of the dhcp-relay vsx active-active command?
The syntax of the dhcp-relay vsx active-active command is: dhcp-relay vsx active-active.

document_title: Ip Services Guide (6300, 6400)

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the dhcp-smart-relay command do?

The dhcp-smart-relay command enables DHCP Smart Relay on the device and on all the interfaces where IP helper addresses are configured. Disabled by default at the device level. Not supported on the management interface. The no form of this command disables DHCP Smart Relay. NOTE Prior to enabling DHCP Smart Relay, enable IP helper address configuration and configure secondary IP addresses on the interface. Syntax: dhcp-smart-relay.

document_title: Ip Services Guide (6300, 6400)

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what is the syntax of the dhcp-smart-relay command?
The syntax of the dhcp-smart-relay command is: dhcp-smart-relay.

document_title: Ip Services Guide (6300, 6400)

To configure the DHCPv6 relay agent, follow the documented procedure: 1. Enable the DHCPv6 relay agent with the command dhcpv6-relay 2. Configure one or more IP helper addresses with the command IPv6 helper-address. This.

document_title: Ip Services Guide (6300, 6400)

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the dhcpv6-relay vsx active-active command do?

The dhcpv6-relay vsx active-active command enables DHCPv6 relay active-active mode in VSX. When enabled, DHCPv6 relay is active on both VSX Primary and Secondary peers. DHCPv6 client requests are processed on the same VSX peer that receives them. A VSX peer that receives DHCPv6 client request packets from its peer silently ignores them. By default, this feature is disabled, and DHCPv6 request packets are forwarded to the configured helper addresses only by the VSX primary. Public dhcpv6-relay vsx active-active 159 The no form of this command disables DHCPv6 relay active-active mode, and DHCPv6 request packets are forwarded to configured helper addresses only by the VSX primary. Syntax: dhcpv6-relay vsx active-active.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the bootp <REMOTE-URL> command do?

The bootp <REMOTE-URL> command sets the BOOTP options that are returned by the DHCPv4 server for the current pool. BOOTP provides a way to distribute an IP address and boot image file to client stations. The DHCPv4 server returns the IP address and the location of the boot image file, which must be stored on an external TFTP server. The no form of this command disables support for BOOTP. <REMOTE-URL> tftp://{<IP> | <HOST>}/<FILE> Specifies the name and location of a BOOTP file on a TFTP server in the format: • <IP>: Specifies the IP address of the TFTP server hosti ng the file in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), where x is a decimal nu mber from 0 to 255. You can remove leading zeros. For exa mple, the address 192.169.005.100 becomes 192.168.5.100. Syntax: bootp <REMOTE-URL>.

For example, the address 192.169.0 05.100 becomes 192.168.5.100. Separate addresses with a space. A maximum of four IP addresses can be defined. <IPv4-ADDR-LIST>. messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the lease {<TIME> | infinite} command do?

The lease {<TIME> | infinite} command sets the length of the DHCPv4 lease time for the current pool. The lease time determines how long an IP address is valid before a DHCPv4 client must request that it be renewed. The no form of this command returns the DHCPv4 lease time to its default value 1 hour. Sets the DHCPv4 lease time. Format: DD: HH: MM. Default: 01: 0 0: 00. <TIME> Public lease 187 Sets the DHCPv4 lease time to infinite. This means that addres ses do not need to be renewed. Sets the lease time for DHCPv4 server pool primary-pool on VRF primary to 12 hours. switch(config)# dhcp-server vrf primary switch(config-dhcp-server)# pool primary-pool switch(config-dhcp-server-pool)# lease 00: 12: 00 Deletes the lease time for DHCPv4 server pool primary-pool on VRF primary. Syntax: lease {<TIME> | infinite}.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the pool <POOL-NAME> command do?

The pool <POOL-NAME> command creates a DHCPv4 server pool for the current VRF and switches to the config-dhcp-server-pool context for it. Multiple pools, each with a distinct range, can be assigned to a VRF. A maximum of 64 pools (IPv4 and IPv6), 64 address ranges, and 8182 clients are supported on the switch across all VRFs. The no form of this command deletes the specified DHCPv4 server pool. <POOL-NAME> Specifies the DHCPv4 pool name. A maximum of 64 pools (IP v 4 and IPv6) are supported across VRFs on the switch. Range: 1 to 32 printable ASCII characters. First character must be a letter or number. Public pool 193. Syntax: pool <POOL-NAME>.

A maximum of 64 ranges are supported per switch across all VRFs. The no form of this command deletes the address range for the current pool. Public range 194 <LOW-IPv4-ADDR> <HIGH-IPv4-ADDR> prefix-len <MASK> Specifies the lowest IP address in the pool in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), where x is a decimal number from 0 to 255. Specifies the highest IP address in the pool in IPv4 format (x.x. x.x), where x is a decimal number from 0 to 255. Specifies the number of bits in the address mask in CIDR format (x), where x is a decimal number from 0 to 32. Syntax: range <LOW-IPv4-ADDR> <HIGH-IPv4-ADDR> [prefix-len <MASK>].

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the static-bind {ip <IPv4-ADDR>}|{ mac <MAC-ADDR>} [hostname <HOST>] command do?

The static-bind {ip <IPv4-ADDR>}|{ mac <MAC-ADDR>} [hostname <HOST>] command creates a static binding that associates an IP address in the current pool with a specific MAC address. This causes the DHCPv4 server to only assign the specified IP address to a client station with the specified MAC address. The no form of this command removes the specified binding. Specifies an IP address in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), where x is a de cimal number from 0 to 255. The IP address must be within the address range defined for the current pool. Specifies a client station MAC address (xx: xx: xx: xx: xx: xx), where x is a hexadecimal number from 0 to F. Specifies the host name of the client station. Range: 1 to 255 pr intable ASCII characters <IPv4-ADDR> mac <MAC-ADDR> hostname <HOST>. Syntax: static-bind {ip <IPv4-ADDR>}|{ mac <MAC-ADDR>} [hostname <HOST>].

Clears leases for all VRFs. <IPv6-ADDR> vrf <VRF-NAME> Clears the lease for a specific client on a specific VRF. Specify t he client address in IPv6 format (xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: x xxx: xxxx: xxxx), where x is a hexadecimal number from 0 to F. You can use two colons (: :) to represent consecutive zeros (but only once), remove leading zeros, and collapse a hextet of four zeros to a single 0. For example, this address 2222: 0000: 3 333: 0000: 0000: 0000: 4444: 0055 becomes 2222: 0: 3333: : 4444: 55. Public clear dhcpv6-server leases 202 vrf <VRF-NAME> Clears leases for a specific VRF. Syntax: clear dhcpv6-server leases [all-vrfs | <IPv6-ADDR> vrf <VRF-NAME>] | vrf.

section: 4. Which VLAN is assigned a DHCP address?

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the guide say about 4. Which VLAN is assigned a DHCP address? ?

4. Which VLAN is assigned a DHCP address? : The factory default VLAN to which the DHCP address is assigned is VLAN1. Users can configure the VLAN for DHCP allocation on platforms supporting multiple VLAN configurations. Only a single VLAN can support DHCP assignments. Public FAQ 220 5. Can DHCP assignments be disabled in an HPE Aruba Networking AOS-CX switch? Use the no ip dhcp command on the required VLAN interface or management VRF port you can disable the DHCP assignments in an HPE Aruba Networking AOS-CX switch. A static configuration option is available on both ports. 6. Can IP DHCP switch assignment be carried out on a Routed only Port (RoP)? No, RoP is not supported. Only VLAN and OOBM ports can be allocated an address with DHCP. 7. Can an HPE Aruba Networking AOS-CX switch support DHCP IPv6 Allocation? Yes, an HPE Aruba Networking AOS-CX switch can support DHCP IPv6 allocation. However, for Zero Touch Provisioning (ZTP), IPv4 is required. 8.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the dhcp-snooping event-log client command do?

The dhcp-snooping event-log client command this command enables or disables dhcp-snooping client level event logs that help with client telemetry on a remote management station such as HPE Aruba Networking Central. By default, client level event logs are disabled. The no form of this command disables client-level event logs for DHCP snooping after they are enabled. View these logged DHCP snooping events by issuing the command show events -c dhcp-snooping. NOTE For additional information on DHCP-related event logging, please refer to the Event Log Message Reference Guide. Syntax: dhcp-snooping event-log client.

document_title: Ip Services Guide (6300, 6400)

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the dhcp-snooping external-storage volume <VOL-NAME> file <FILE-NAME> command do?

The dhcp-snooping external-storage volume <VOL-NAME> file <FILE-NAME> command configures external storage to be used for backing up IP bindings (used by DHCP snooping) to a file. When configured, the switch stores all the IP bindings in an external storage file so that they are retained after the switch restarts. When the switch restarts, it reads the IP bindings from the configured external storage file to populate its local cache. NOTE When both external storage and flash storage are configured to store DHCP snooping IP bindings, the external storage takes priority, and is used exclusively until it becomes unconfigured, at which time flash storage (if configured) is used. Later, if external storage is configured again, flash storage stops and external storage resumes. The no form of this command disables the saving of IP bindings in an external storage file. Syntax: dhcp-snooping external-storage volume <VOL-NAME> file <FILE-NAME>.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the dhcp-snooping trust command do?

The dhcp-snooping trust command enables DHCP snooping trust on the selected port. Only server packets received on trusted ports are forwarded. All the ports are untrusted by default. The no form of the command disables DHCP snooping trust on the selected port. Syntax: dhcp-snooping trust.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the dhcp-snooping tunnel vxlan trust command do?

The dhcp-snooping tunnel vxlan trust command enables dhcp-snooping trust on all VxLAN tunnels. The no form of the command to marks all VxLAN tunnels as untrusted. By default, all VxLAN tunnel interfaces are trusted. When trust is disabled on VxLAN tunnel interfaces: • DHCP broadcast packets are not forwarded on VxLAN tunnels. • DHCP server packets received on VxLAN tunnel interfaces are discarded. Syntax: dhcp-snooping tunnel vxlan trust.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the dhcp-snooping verify mac command do?

The dhcp-snooping verify mac command this command enables verification of the hardware address field in DHCP client packets. When enabled, the DHCP client hardware address field and the source MAC address must be the same for packets received on untrusted ports or else the packet is dropped. This DHCP snooping MAC verification is enabled by default. The no form of the command disables DHCP snooping MAC verification. Syntax: dhcp-snooping verify mac.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the dhcpv6-snooping flash-storage [delay <DELAY>] command do?

The dhcpv6-snooping flash-storage [delay <DELAY>] command configures switch flash storage to be used for backing up client IP bindings (used by DHCPv6 snooping). When flash storage is configured (and external storage is not already configured for this purpose), the switch stores the IP bindings in switch flash storage. When the switch restarts, it reads the IP bindings from the switch flash storage to populate its local cache. Writing the IP bindings to flash storage only occurs after the configured delay and if there has been a change in client IP bindings. Writing is skipped when client IP bindings have not changed since the previous write. Omitting delay <DELAY> sets the default delay of 900 seconds. Syntax: dhcpv6-snooping flash-storage [delay <DELAY>].

The dhcpv6-snooping trust command enables DHCPv6 snooping trust on the selected interface. Only server packets received on trusted interfaces are forwarded. All the interfaces are untrusted by default. The no form of the command disables DHCPv6 snooping trust on the selected interface. config-if. Syntax: dhcpv6-snooping trust.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the dhcpv6-snooping tunnel vxlan trust command do?

The dhcpv6-snooping tunnel vxlan trust command enables DHCPv6-snooping trust on all VxLAN tunnels. The no form of the command to marks all VxLAN tunnels as untrusted. By default, all VxLAN tunnel interfaces are trusted. When trust is disabled on VxLAN tunnel interfaces: • DHCP broadcast packets are not forwarded on VxLAN tunnels. • DHCP server packets received on VxLAN tunnel interfaces are discarded. Syntax: dhcpv6-snooping tunnel vxlan trust.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the preference [minimum | maximum ] <VALUE> command do?

The preference [minimum | maximum ] <VALUE> command enables a DHCPv6 snooping guard policy to allow or deny the DHCPv6 servers in the specified server preference range. If not configured the minimum preference is set to 0 and maximum preference is set to 255. The no form of the command removes the server preference limits on the specified DHCPv6 snooping guard policy. minimum <VALUE> maximum <VALUE>. Syntax: preference [minimum | maximum ] <VALUE>.

section: 5. Server and client are connected to same the VLAN

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the guide say about 5. Server and client are connected to same the VLAN?

5. Server and client are connected to same the VLAN: a. Check if the server is reachable on the same VLAN as the client. b. If the server is on a different L3 network, configure DHCP-Relay to forward the packets between the client and server. 6. Authorized server configurations-When configured, the server assigning IP address should be one of the IP addresses from the authorized server list. In DHCP snooping IPv6, if DHCP Relay is between a test device and a server, the authorised server configuration should include a link to the local IP address of the relay interface. This is because, while forwarding the server's response to the client, it will stamp a link to the local IP address as the source IP of the packet.

topic: 5. Server and client are connected to same the VLAN

ND snooping must be enabled both globally and on this VLAN before this prefix list configuration takes effect. The no form of this command removes the prefix list configuration for the selected VLAN and IPv6 address. Specifies the IPv6 address. <IPv6-ADDR>. Syntax: nd-snooping prefix-list <IPv6-ADDR>.

For all interfaces on which this command is not run, the default max bindings applies. The no form of the command reverts max bindings for the selected interface to its default. Public nd-snooping max-bindings 306 Specifies the maximum number of ND bindings. You can use the show capacities command to see the maximum available for yo ur switch model. <MAX-BINDINGS>. Syntax: nd-snooping max-bindings <MAX-BINDINGS>.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the nd-snooping ra-guard [log] command do?

The nd-snooping ra-guard [log] command this command enables Routing Advertisement (RA) guard on the selected VLAN. When enabled, ingress Routing Advertisement (RA) and Routing Redirect (RR) packets on the selected VLAN are blocked on untrusted ports. The packets are forwarded when received on trusted ports. The no form of the command disables RA guard on the VLAN. NOTE ND snooping must be enabled in both the global context and the config-vlan context before this command can be used. Logs messages along with drop functionality. Syntax: nd-snooping ra-guard [log].

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the nd-snooping ra-drop command do?

The nd-snooping ra-drop command this command enables Routing Advertisement (RA) drop on the selected VLAN. When enabled, ingress RA packets on the selected VLAN are blocked on both trusted and untrusted ports. When disabled, RA Public nd-snooping ra-drop 310 packets are forwarded on the selected VLAN with ND snooping trusted port validation. RA drop is disabled by default. NOTE ND snooping must be enabled in both the config context and the config-vlan context before this command can be used. The no form of the command disables ND snooping RA drop on the selected VLAN. Syntax: nd-snooping ra-drop.

The managed-config-flag [on | off] command enables the verification of the advertised manage configuration flag. Verifies that the advertised managed address configuration flag is On or Off based on the configured value. The no form of the command disables the manage configuration flag verification. NOTE ND snooping must be enabled in both the global context and the config-vlan context before this command can be used. on Verifies that the advertised managed address configuration fla g is On. Public managed-config-flag 323 off Verifies that the advertised managed address configuration fla g is Off. Syntax: managed-config-flag [on | off].

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the match access-list <ACL-NAME> command do?

The match access-list <ACL-NAME> command configures the access list to an RA guard policy. The access list has to be created with the desired match criteria before adding it into RA guard policy. Advertised packets are verified for the match criteria when an RA guard policy with matched access list is enabled on a trusted port or VLANs. The no form of the command removes the access list from the RA guard policy. NOTE ND snooping must be enabled in both the global context and the config-vlan context before this command can be used. Specifies the name of the access list to be matched. <ACL-NAME>. Syntax: match access-list <ACL-NAME>.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the match prefix-list <PREFIX-LIST-NAME> command do?

The match prefix-list <PREFIX-LIST-NAME> command configures a prefix-list for the RA guard policy. Advertised prefixes in RA packets are compared against the configured prefix-list and if there is no match, the RA packets are dropped. If the RA prefix list is not configured, this check is not performed. The no form of the command removes the prefix list from the RA guard policy. NOTE ND snooping must be enabled in both the global context and the config-vlan context before this command can be used. Specifies the name of the prefix list to be matched. <PREFIX-LIST-NAME>. Syntax: match prefix-list <PREFIX-LIST-NAME>.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip address command do?

The ip address command sets the local IP address of a GRE tunnel. This address identifies the tunnel interface for routing. It must be on the same subnet as the tunnel address assigned on the remote device. The no form of this command deletes the local IP address assigned to a GRE tunnel. <IPv4-ADDR> Specifies the tunnel IP address in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), where x is a decimal number from 0 to 255. You can remove leading ze ros. For example, the address 192.169.005.100 becomes 192. 168.5.100. Public ip address 366 Specifies the number of bits in the address mask in CIDR format (x), where x is a decimal number from 0 to 32. <MASK>. Syntax: ip address <IPv4-ADDR>/<MASK>.

document_title: Ip Services Guide (6300, 6400)

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what is the syntax of the ip address command?

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 address command do?

The IPv6 address command sets the local IP address of an IPv6 to IPv4 tunnel or of an IPv6 to IPv6 tunnel. This address identifies the tunnel interface for routing. It must be on the same subnet as the tunnel address assigned on the remote device. The no form of this command deletes the local IP address assigned to an IPv6 to IPv4 tunnel. Specifies the tunnel IP address in IPv6 format (xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx), where x is a hexadecimal numbe r from 0 to F. Specifies the number of bits in the address mask in CIDR format (x), where x is a decimal number from 0 to 32. <IPv6-ADDR> <MASK>. Syntax: IPv6 address <IPv6-ADDR>/<MASK>.

document_title: Ip Services Guide (6300, 6400)

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what is the syntax of the IPv6 address command?
The syntax of the IPv6 address command is: IPv6 address <IPv6-ADDR>/<MASK>

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip mtu command do?

The ip mtu command sets the MTU (maximum transmission unit) for an IP interface. The default value is 1500 bytes. The no form of this command sets the MTU to the default value of 1500 bytes. Specifies the MTU in bytes. Range: 1, 280 bytes to 9, 192 bytes. <VALUE>. Syntax: ip mtu <VALUE>.

Specifies the tunnel IP address in IPv6 format (xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx), where x is a hexadecimal numbe r from 0 to F. This is optional in the no form of the command. <IPv6-ADDR>. Syntax: source IPv6 <IPv6-ADDR>.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the vrf attach command do?

The vrf attach command assigns an IP tunnel to a VRF. By default, all tunnels are automatically assigned to the default VRF when they are created. The no form of this command assigns a tunnel to the default VRF (default). Specifies the VRF name to which to assign the tunnel. <VRF-NAME>. Syntax: vrf attach <VRF-NAME>.

document_title: Ip Services Guide (6300, 6400)

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what is the syntax of the vrf attach command?

document_title: Ip Services Guide (6300, 6400)

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the no IPv4 source-binding <VLAN-ID> command do?

The no IPv4 source-binding <VLAN-ID> command adds static IPv4 client source binding information to the switch IP binding database. Although DHCPv4 snooping is often used to dynamically populate the binding database, this command is available for manually adding entries to the switch IP binding database. NOTE Statically configured IP binding information supersedes any dynamically collected binding information for the same client. The no form of this command removes the specified binding that was statically configured with the IPv4 source-binding command. The no form has no effect on bindings that were dynamically configured with DHCPv4 snooping. Specifies the ID of an existing VLAN on which the client is conn ected. Range: 1 to 4094.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the guide say about IPv4 source-lockdown hardware retry <VLAN-ID>?

IPv4 source-lockdown hardware retry <VLAN-ID>: <IPv4-ADDR> Description Retries the IPv4 source lockdown hardware programming for a client identified by VLAN and IPv4 address. Parameter Description Specifies the ID of an existing VLAN on which the client is conn ected. Range: 1 to 4094. Public IPv4 source-lockdown hardware retry 389 Description Specifies the client IPv4 unicast address. Parameter.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the show IPv4 source-binding command do?

The show IPv4 source-binding command shows all IPv4 static source binding information irrespective of source lockdown configuration.. Public show IPv4 source-binding 390 Shows the output from the VSX peer switch. If the switches do not have the VSX configuration or the ISL is down, the output f rom the VSX peer switch is not displayed. This parameter is avai lable on switches that support VSX. On the HPE Aruba Networking 6400 Switch Series, interface identification differs. Showing all IPv4 source binding information: switch# show IPv4 source-binding. Syntax: show IPv4 source-binding [vsx-peer].

document_title: Ip Services Guide (6300, 6400)

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, how do you configure the DNS client?

To configure the DNS client, follow the documented procedure: 1. Configure one or more DNS name servers with the command ip dns server 2. To resolve DNS requests by appending a domain name to the requests, either configure a single domain 3. To use static name resolution for certain hosts, associate an IP address to a host with the command ip 4. Review your DNS configuration settings with the command show ip dns. Commands shown in the procedure include: VRF Name: default.

document_title: Ip Services Guide (6300, 6400)

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip dns host command do?

The ip dns host command associates a static IP address with a hostname. The DNS client returns this IP address instead of querying a DNS server for an IP address for the hostname. Up to six hosts can be defined. If no VRF is defined, the default VRF is used. The no form of this command removes a static IP address associated with a hostname. host <HOST-NAME> Specifies the name of a host. Length: 1 to 256 characters. <IP-ADDR> Specifies an IP address in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), where x is a de cimal number from 0 to 255, or IPv6 format (xxxx: xxxx: x xxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx), where x is a hexa decimal number from 0 to F. vrf <VRF-NAME> Specifies a VRF name. Default: default. Syntax: ip dns host <HOST-NAME>.

document_title: Ip Services Guide (6300, 6400)

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what is the syntax of the ip dns host command?
The syntax of the ip dns host command is: ip dns host <HOST-NAME>

document_title: Ip Services Guide (6300, 6400)
section: Step 2: Configuring L3 gateway in switch A

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the guide say about Step 2: Configuring L3 gateway in switch A?

Step 2: Configuring L3 gateway in switch A: L3-GW(config)# vlan 100 L3-GW(config-vlan-100)# exit L3-GW(config)# interface 1/1/25 L3-GW(config-if)# vlan trunk allowed 100 L3-GW(config-if)# no shutdown L3-GW(config-if)# exit L3-GW(config)# interface vlan 100 L3-GW(config-if-vlan)# ip address 192.168.1.1/24 Now the clients will be able to ping the gateway IP; but will not be able to communicate with each other due to the isolation enforced with private VLAN. As expected, the ping from client 1 to client 2 will fail in the above example, although the clients are in the same subnet.

topic: Step 2: Configuring L3 gateway in switch A

document_title: Ip Services Guide (6300, 6400)
section: Step 3: Configuring local proxy ARP in switch A

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the guide say about Step 3: Configuring local proxy ARP in switch A?

Step 3: Configuring local proxy ARP in switch A: If local proxy ARP is enabled in the L3 gateway, the L3 gateway responds to ARP requests for addresses in the same subnet. In other words, the L3 gateway will be doing proxy for ARP resolution. L3-GW(config)# no ip icmp redirect L3-GW(config)# interface vlan 100 L3-GW(config-if-vlan)# ip local-proxy-arp Public ARP 433 With this, the L3 communication will be enabled between client 1 and client 2. Now the ping from client 1 to client 2 will be successful in the above example. The ARP table in client 1 will be populated with the MAC of L3 gateway; not the MAC of client 2. Similar ARP table entry for client 2 as well. In other words, the L3 gateway is doing proxy for ARP resolution for the local network where clients are isolated at layer 2. Dynamic ARP inspection Dynamic arp inspection is provided as a mechanism for making ARP more secure. NOTE • Dynamic ARP inspection over VXLAN is supported only on the HPE Aruba Networking 6300 Switch Series.

topic: Step 3: Configuring local proxy ARP in switch A

document_title: Ip Services Guide (6300, 6400)

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, how do you configure proxy ARP?

To configure proxy ARP, follow the documented procedure: 1. Switch to configuration context with the command config 2. Switch to an interface with the command interface, or to an interface VLAN with the command i 3. Enable local proxy ARP with the command ip proxy-arp.

document_title: Ip Services Guide (6300, 6400)

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, how do you configure local proxy ARP?

To configure local proxy ARP, follow the documented procedure: 1. Switch to configuration context with the command config 2. Switch to an interface with the command interface, or to an interface VLAN with the command i 3. Enable local proxy ARP with the command ip local-proxy-arp.

document_title: Ip Services Guide (6300, 6400)
section: Configuring dynamic ARP inspection over VXLAN overlay

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, how do you configure dynamic ARP inspection over VXLAN overlay?

To configure dynamic ARP inspection over VXLAN overlay, follow the documented procedure: 1. Configure VXLAN overlay setup to establish the VxLAN tunnel. For more information, see AOS-CX 2. Configure ARP inspection in the VLAN context with the command arp inspection 3. Configure the port as trusted or untrusted with the command arp inspection trust 4. Validate the ARP inspection configuration with the command show arp inspection.

task: Configuring dynamic ARP inspection over VXLAN overlay

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the arp inspection trust command do?

The arp inspection trust command configures the interface as a trusted. All interfaces are untrusted by default. The no form of this command returns the interface to the default state (untrusted). Syntax: arp inspection trust.

document_title: Ip Services Guide (6300, 6400)

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what is the syntax of the arp inspection trust command?

document_title: Ip Services Guide (6300, 6400)

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the clear arp command do?

The clear arp command clears IPv4 and IPv6 neighbor entries from the ARP table. If you do not specify any VRF or port parameters, ARP table entries are cleared for the default VRF. Specifies a port on the switch. For example: 1/1/1. ip <A. B. C. D>|all] IPv6 <X: X: : X: X>|all vrf (Optional) Include an IP address to clear neighbor entries for th at specific address, or use the all parameter to clear entries for all IP addresses. (Optional) Include an IPv6 address to clear neighbor entries for that specific address, or use the all parameter to clear entries fo r all IPv6 addresses. Clears IPv4 and IPv6 neighbor entries for the specified VRF or for all VRFs. If no VRF is specified he default VRF is cleared. all-vrfs Clear neighbor entries for all VRFs Clear neighbor entries for the specified VRF. Syntax: clear arp.

document_title: Ip Services Guide (6300, 6400)

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what is the syntax of the clear arp command?
The syntax of the clear arp command is: clear arp.

document_title: Ip Services Guide (6300, 6400)

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 local-proxy-nd exclude command do?

The IPv6 local-proxy-nd exclude command enables IP exemption for local proxy ND for the specified interface and IPv6 address. When configured, the switch will not respond to neighbor solicitation (NS) messages for the specified IPv6 address. The maximum IPv6 addresses can be exempted from Local Proxy ND per interface are 256. The maximum IPv6 addresses can be exempted from Local proxy ND across all interfaces are 256. The no version of this command disables IP exemption for local proxy ND for the specified interface and IPv6 address, while local proxy ND remains configured on the interface. After executing this command, the switch will respond to neighbor solicitation (NS) messages for the specified IPv6 address. IPv6 address to exclude from local proxy ND. Syntax: IPv6 local-proxy-nd exclude {<IPv6-address>}.

document_title: Ip Services Guide (6300, 6400)

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what is the syntax of the IPv6 local-proxy-nd exclude command?
The syntax of the IPv6 local-proxy-nd exclude command is: IPv6 local-proxy-nd exclude {<IPv6-address>}.

document_title: Ip Services Guide (6300, 6400)

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 neighbor mac command do?

The IPv6 neighbor mac command specifies a permanent static neighbor entry in the ARP table (for IPv6 neighbors). The no form of this command deletes a permanent static neighbor entry from the ARP table. Specifies an IP address in IPv6 format (xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: x xxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx), where x is a hexadecimal number from 0 to F. Range: 4096 to 131072. Default: 131072. Public IPv6 neighbor mac 456 Specifies the MAC address of the neighbor (xx: xx: xx: xx: xx: xx), where x is a hexadecimal number from 0 to F. Range: 4096 to 131072. Default: 131072. On the HPE Aruba Networking 6400 Switch Series, interface identification differs. Creates a static ARP entry on interface 1/1/1. switch(config)# interface 1/1/1 switch(config-if)# arp IPv6 neighbor 2001: 0db8: 85a3: : 8a2e: 0370: 7334 mac 00: 50: 56: 96: df: c8. Syntax: IPv6 neighbor <IPv6-ADDR> mac <MAC-ADDR>.

document_title: Ip Services Guide (6300, 6400)

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what is the syntax of the IPv6 neighbor mac command?
The syntax of the IPv6 neighbor mac command is: IPv6 neighbor <IPv6-ADDR> mac <MAC-ADDR>

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the flow record <record-name> command do?

The flow record <record-name> command creates or modifies a flow record and switches to the config-flow-record context for the flow record. Define data to be included in a flow record by configuring flow record match and collect fields. A flow record defines match (key) fields and collection (non-key) fields. Customers configure flow records with match (key) fields and collect (non-key) fields. Match fields are the set of fields that define a flow, such as IP address or UDP port. Collect fields are the set of fields that identify information to collect for a flow, such as packet and byte counters. Traffic with matching attributes (for example, traffic coming from the same interface, sent to the same destination with the same protocol) are classified as a single flow. Syntax: flow record <record-name>.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the flow-tracking command do?

The flow-tracking command configuresFlow tracking for TCP and UDP flows, and optionally, ICMP flows. The no form of this command deletes the flow tracking configuration context. In order to optimize the flow removal process, flows that have aged-out are flushed in batches. A flow that has aged out is flushed only when the next batch processes. This can cause some flows to stay inactive for a slightly longer time than the value configured here. EnablesFlow tracking. Configures an age-out time for ICMP flows, in seconds. Range: 10 to 86400. Default: 15. Configures global concurrent flow limit for flow tracking enable d interfaces. Range: For 6300 and 6400 Switch series, 64 to 25 000.. Default: none. flow ConfiguresFlow parameters. statistics ConfiguresFlow statistics. enable EnablesFlow statistics collection. Syntax: flow-tracking.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip IPv6 flow monitor (interface) command do?

The ip IPv6 flow monitor (interface) command enable flow monitoring on inbound and outbound interfaces by assigning a flow monitor to that interface. Only physical interfaces and LAG interfaces can be monitored. A flow monitor cannot be applied to an interface that is part of a LAG. If an unsupported application is attempted, an error message will be displayed. If the flow monitor is associated with a flow record that contains application fields as collect fields, then Application Recognition should be enabled on the same interface. The [no] form of command disables the flow monitoring. Syntax: [no] ip|IPv6 flow monitor (interface).

The show flow exporter command displaysFlow exporter statistics, configuration and status. When no exporter name is specified, the output of this command displays information for all flow exporters. The output of this command can indicate the following status types: Public show flow exporter 59 • Accepted • Rejected (Internal error: exporter does not exist) • Rejected (Internal error: destination type does not exist) • Rejected (Destination type is hostname or IP address, but no destination is specified) • Rejected (Destination type is hostname or IP address, but the specified hostname or IP address is invalid) • Rejected (Destination type is Traffic Insight, but no destination is specified) • Rejected (Destination type is Traffic Insight, but the specified Traffic Insight instance does not exist) • Rejected (Destinatio. Syntax: show flow exporter [<name>].

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the apply congestion-event profile command do?

The apply congestion-event profile command applies a congestion event profile to an interface. The no form removes the congestion event profile from the interface. Specifies the name of a congestion event profile. Range: 1 to 64 alphanumeric characters, including period (.), un derscore (_), and hyphen (-). In order for the congestion event profile entry associated with an applied congestion event profile to be effective, the interface needs to be configured for queue-monitor. Once the queue statistics are being monitored, apply the congestion event profile to the interface to begin the process of actively checking the monitored queue statistics for congestion events based on the thresholds configured in the applied congestion event profile. Syntax: apply congestion-event profile [<NAME>].

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip-sla responder command do?

The ip-sla responder command selects the IP-SLA responder. The responder can be configured for udp-echo, tcp-connect, udp-jitter-voip type. It requires the SLA name, SLA type, and port number as arguments. The no form of this command removes the IP-SLA responder. Specifies the IP-SLA responder name. Length: 1 to 64 chara cters. <SLA-NAME> udp-echo tcp-connect Enables responder for udp-echo probes. Selects TCP connect as the IP-SLA test mechanism. udp-jitter-voip Selects VOIP jitter as the IP-SLA test mechanism. Specifies the port number to listen for IP-SLA probes. Range: 1 to 65535. <PORT-NUM> source {<SOURCE-IPv4- ADDR>|<SOURCE-IPv6-ADDR>| <IFNAME>} Selects the source IPv4 or IPv6 address for SLA probes or the s ource interface to use for sending IP-SLA probes. vrf <VRF-NAME> Specifies the name of the VRF to use. Syntax: ip-sla responder <SLA-NAME> (udp-echo | tcp-connect | udp-jitter-voip).

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what monitoring behavior is documented for Mirroring on a VSX configuration?

Mirroring on a VSX configuration: On 6400 switch series with a VSX configuration, north to south traffic may enter one switch, then cross the ISL to the other switch to reach its destination. If both 6400 switches have the following mirror configuration and the analyzer is connected to 1/1/10 on both 6400s, then the packet is mirrored by each 6400 switch, and two copies are sent to the analyzer. mirror session 1 source vlan 1 both source vlan 2 both... destination 1/1/10.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the destination cpu command do?

The destination cpu command the command causes the mirror session to transmit mirrored packets to the switch CPU. This destination may be configured for multiple sessions, however only one such configured session may be active at a given time. The diagnostic utility Tshark may be used to view and capture packets transmitted to the CPU through this route. Ctrl+C must be entered to terminate a Tshark capture session. More details can be found in the Supportability Guide. Public destination cpu 187 The no form of this command will immediately stops mirroring traffic to the CPU, but will not remove any sources from the mirror configuration. Syntax: destination cpu.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the mirror session <SESSION-ID> command do?

The mirror session <SESSION-ID> command creates a mirroring session configuration context or enters an existing mirroring session configuration context. From this context, you can enter commands to configure and enable or disable the mirroring session. The no form of this command removes an existing mirroring session from the configuration. Specifies the session identifier. Range: 1 to 4 <SESSION-ID>. Syntax: mirror session <SESSION-ID>.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what monitoring behavior is documented for gre Generic Routing Encapsulation?

gre Generic Routing Encapsulation: icmpv6 Internet Control Message Protocol Version 6 IPv6 Any Internet Protocol ospf Open Shortest Path First (version 3) pim Protocol Independent Multicast sctp Stream Control Transmission Protocol tcp Transmission Control Protocol udp User Datagram Protocol General Limitations • Only 3 pcap files are allowed at one time. Creating a fourth pcap file will replace the oldest one. • Packet capture is only supported on physical ports. • Only one packet-capture session can be active a time. • A packet-capture session cannot be active at the same time as a mirror session with destination CPU. • Packets captured by packet-capture sessions will not include the Ethernet frame check sequence in the resulting capture.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the lldp trap enable command do?

The lldp trap enable command enables sending SNMP traps for LLDP related events from a particular interface. LLDP trap generation is enabled by default on all the interfaces and has to be disabled for interfaces on which traps are not required to be generated. The no form of this command disables the LLDP trap generation. NOTE LLDP trap generation is disabled by default at the global level and must be enabled before any LLDP traps are sent. Syntax: lldp trap enable.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the snmpv3 context <NAME> vrf <VRF-NAME> [community <STRING>] command do?

The snmpv3 context <NAME> vrf <VRF-NAME> [community <STRING>] command creates an SNMP v3 context on the specified VRF. The no form of this command removes the specified SNMP context. Specifies the name of the context. Range: 1 to 32 printable ASC II characters, excluding space and question mark (?). Public snmpv3 context 295 vrf <VRF-NAME> Specifies the VRF associated with the context. Default: default. community <STRING> Specifies the SNMP community string associated with the con text. Range: 1 to 32 printable ASCII characters, excluding space and question mark. Default: public. Syntax: snmpv3 context <NAME> vrf <VRF-NAME> [community <STRING>].

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the snmpv3 engine-id <ENGINE-ID> command do?

The snmpv3 engine-id <ENGINE-ID> command configures the SNMP v3 SNMP engine-id allowing an administrator to configure a unique SNMP engine-id for the switch. This engine-id is used by the NMS management tool to identify and distinguish multiple switches on the same network. The no form of this command restores the default engine-id, created by the switch using a combination of the enterprise OID value and the switch's MAC address. SNMP v3 SNMP engine-id in colon separated hexadecimal n otation. <ENGINE-ID>. Syntax: snmpv3 engine-id <ENGINE-ID>.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the snmpv3 security-level {auth | auth-privacy} command do?

The snmpv3 security-level {auth | auth-privacy} command configures the SNMP v3 security level. The security level determines which SMNPv3 users defined by the command snmpv3 user are able to connect. The no form of this command changes the security level as follows: • no snmpv3 security-level auth: Sets the security level to auth-privacy. • no snmpv3 security-level auth-privacy: Sets the security level to no authentication or privacy, allowing any SNMP user to connect. SNMP v3 users that support authentication, or authentication a nd privacy are allowed. Only SNMP v3 users with both authentication and privacy are al lowed. This is the highest level of SNMP v3 security. Default. Syntax: snmpv3 security-level {auth | auth-privacy}.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the snmp-server agent-port command do?

The snmp-server agent-port command sets the UDP port number that the SNMP master agent uses to communicate. UDP port 161 is the default port. The no form of this command sets the SNMP master agent port to the default value. Specifies the UDP port number that the SNMP master agent wil l use. Range: 1 to 65535. Default: 161. <PORT>. Syntax: snmp-server agent-port <PORT>.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the snmp-server community command do?

The snmp-server community command adds an SNMP v1/SNMP v2c community string. A community string is like a password that controls read/ write access to the SNMP agent. A network management program must supply this name when attempting Public snmp-server community 301 to get SNMP information from the switch. A maximum of 10 community strings are supported. Once you create your own community string, the default community string (public) is deleted. The no form of this command removes the specified SNMP v1/SNMP v2c community string. When no community string exists, a default community string with the value public is automatically defined. Specifies the SNMP v1/SNMP v2c community string. Range: 1 to 32 printable ASCII characters, excluding space and question ma rk. Syntax: snmp-server community <STRING>.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the snmp-server vrf command do?

The snmp-server vrf command configures a VRF on which the SNMP agent listens for incoming requests. By default, the SNMP agent does not listen on any VRF. 4100i, 6000, and 6100 only support default VRF. The SNMP agent can listen on multiple VRFs. The no form of this command stops the SNMP agent from listening for incoming requests on the specified VRF. Specifies the name of a VRF. <VRF-NAME>. Syntax: snmp-server vrf <VRF-NAME>.

The snmp-server trap port-security command enables SNMP port-security violation traps on the system. Port-security violation traps are enabled by default. The no form of this command disables the SNMP port-security violation traps on the system. Specifies SNMP traps for port-security. Enabling the SNMP port-security violation traps on the system: switch(config)# snmp-server trap port-security Disabling the SNMP port-security violation traps on the system: switch(config)# no snmp-server trap port-security Public snmp-server trap port-security 331. messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the queue-monitor command do?

The queue-monitor command this command enables the queue monitoring feature, allowing the switch to collect queue statistics. then display this information as a queue statistics history. These aggregate statistics can be used to monitor network health, troubleshoot network issues, and identify normal network behavior patterns and performance anomalies. This feature is disabled by default, but can be enabled on a maximum of 52 interfaces per switch. Syntax: queue-monitor.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, how do you configure EBGP peering between PE and CE?

To configure EBGP peering between PE and CE, follow the documented procedure: 1. Enter interface facing CE interface <IFNAME> Desired IP address will also need 2. Specify remote CE IP neighbor <IP-ADDRESS> EBGP should be used between CE. Commands shown in the procedure include: vrf attach <VRF-NAME>; vrf <VRF-NAME>.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the clear mpls statistics command do?

The clear mpls statistics command clears MPLS statistics per label for all sessions. | Parameter | | Description | | ingress | | Selects ingress statistics. | | egress | | Selects egress statistics. | Specifies the label for which statistics will be cleared. <LABEL>. Syntax: clear mpls statistics {ingress | egress} <LABEL>.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the crossconnect input <in-label> output <IFNAME> command do?

The crossconnect input <in-label> output <IFNAME> command configures a static LSP binding to swap labels and route to the given next hop. The no form of this command removes the static LSP binding label swap configuration. Specifies the MPLS label to bind. Range: 16-1048575. <in-label> <IFNAME> <IP-ADDR> <out-label> Specifies the egress interface of the binding. Specifies the next hop IP address of the binding. Specifies the MPLS label to apply. Range: 16-1048575. explicit-null Specifies an IETF MPLS IPv4 explicit null label (0). Syntax: crossconnect input <in-label> output <IFNAME>.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the graceful-restart command do?

The graceful-restart command enables LDP graceful restart. Graceful restart is enabled by default. With graceful restart enabled, the MPLS forwarding state will be temporarily retained if the control plane restarts. The switch will wait after losing LDP neighbors before deleting bindings from that neighbor. See mpls ldp graceful restart timers for details. Graceful restart is enabled for LDP sessions only when the LDP setting and the overall router setting are enabled. If either is disabled, then graceful restart will not occur for LDP sessions. Upon being disabled or enabled, any LDP sessions will be restarted, which may result in temporary traffic loss. The no form of this command disables LDP graceful restart. Syntax: graceful-restart.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the router-id (mpls ldp) command do?

The router-id (mpls ldp) command configures MPLS LDP router ID which is the IP address of a loopback interface. The no form of this command removes the MPLS LDP router ID configuration. Public router-id (mpls ldp) 206 Specifies the loopback interface for the MPLS LDP router ID. <IFNAME>. Syntax: router-id <IFNAME> [confirm].

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the static-lsp command do?

The static-lsp command configures MPLS static Label Switched Paths (LSP). The no form of this command removes all static LSP configurations including label range allocation and static LSP binding. Syntax: static-lsp.

section: 3. Is VSX required on PEs for high availability?
source_file: 6300_6400/10_17_1000/MPLS Guide (6400 (ROX44C, ROX45C), 8360 Switch Series).md

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the guide say about 3. Is VSX required on PEs for high availability? ?

3. Is VSX required on PEs for high availability? : VSX is not required on PEs as L3 routing across 2 standalone PEs (from a dual homed CE) provides PE redundancy. 4. How does the L3VPN solution enable site redundancy? Site redundancy can be achieved using dual standalone PE devices connected to the site CE device that requires redundancy instead of using the PE devices in VSX mode. In order to ensure optimal best path selection, BGP configurations should implemented as explained in the examples above with same RD configurations on both PE devices. Public Frequently asked questions 239 5. Are non-L3VPN forwarding deployments supported? Non-L3VPN forwarding deployments are supported with static LSP. Please refer to the l sp label imposition command for configuration details. LDP/BGP need to be configured on devices as explained in the examples above for all L3VPN deployments. 6.

topic: 3. Is VSX required on PEs for high availability?
